summary: Develop 6 competing book concepts from ranked opportunities, score them with a judge panel, refine the winner
logs: Tournament winner: "The Perfect Listener" (124 pts). Runner-up: "The Off-Ramp" (117 pts)

score_table.title: The Perfect Listener
rationales: [editor] This is the one concept where the 2028 wave is a near-certainty — deepfake fraud will be strictly worse when this book ships, so the urgency compounds rather than expires, and the Gift-of-Fear protocols-not-tactics architecture is the correct answer to my staleness allergy. The rep one-liner writes itself ('The Gift of Fear for the deepfake era'), the dual-generation Thanksgiving purchase is a real two-copies-per-family mechanic, and the author package (fraud-beat journalist + AARP/IC3 credential) is genuinely findable in 90 days, unlike ranks 1 and 3. Docked on originality because it is consciously a Haidt/de Becker mashup, and the title needs to die in its first marketing meeting — but that is a fixable problem, which is the kind I like.

[agent] This is the one that starts a heated auction the week it goes out: the Haidt buyer is proven at 2M+ copies, the shelf is verifiably empty, and the 33%-choose-the-bot-over-you stat is a complete morning-show segment. The anti-ban 'out-relate the machine' thesis is sharp enough to survive the proposal process, which most panic books aren't. But I've watched a dozen 'we just need to attach the expert' packages die in the author search, and the settlement-beat journalists are absolutely writing proposals right now — the exposé landing first doesn't kill this, but a clinician-fronted rival does. Score it as the hottest property with the shakiest deed.

[marketer] This is the best publicity machine in the field: the 33%-chose-the-bot-over-you stat is a complete GMA segment in one sentence, the Family AI Agreement is a genuinely engineered viral artifact (the Wait Until 8th playbook, which worked), and SB 243 enforcement plus settlement anniversaries give me bookable pegs on a calendar through 2028. The Haidt buyer is proven, reachable free via parent Facebook groups and school newsletters, and the PTA/district bulk channel is pre-built. I dock pain slightly because the base is largely unaware — I have to manufacture the scare before I can sell the solution, which the 72% stat fortunately does in one chyron. Title is soft at thumbnail ('The Perfect Listener' could be a communication book) but the subtitle rescues it; I'd still test alternatives.
kill_risks: [editor] Free-content sufficiency: AARP, NCOA, and every local news segment give away the safe-word tip at $0, and the fear purchase never converts at the register because the buyer feels 'covered' after a 90-second explainer.

[agent] It's a concept in search of an author: without a contracted Damour/Kennedy-class clinician, this is a memo, not a proposal — and the clinicians who fit already have agents, their own book ideas, and slower clocks than this window allows.

[marketer] Author dependency: without a credentialed, media-fluent clinician attached fast, the package can't book the morning shows that ARE the marketing plan — and a Character.AI-beat journalist's exposé sucks up the media oxygen first.

score_table.title: The Off-Ramp
rationales: [editor] Highest ceiling in the field: the Haidt buyer is proven, the shelf is verifiably empty, the 33%-choose-the-bot-over-you stat is a complete GMA segment, and the schools/bulk channel is pre-built. My two hesitations are structural, not cosmetic — the entire evidence base hangs on one n=1,060 self-report survey, and this is a concept shopping for an author rather than an author with a concept, which is where most of these die. On the 2028 test it mostly passes: companion use will likely be larger, though a post-settlement industry retreat from minors is the scenario that guts the panic before pub. If Damour-or-equivalent signs, I preempt; if not, I pass, because the concept without the clinician is worth nothing.

[agent] The audience math is obscene — 30M+ users who were paying $1,200/month — and the coverage-collapse news cycle is a publicist's dream with an annual open-enrollment re-detonation. But originality is weaker than the pitch admits: BenBella's Wyatt/Hill title already planted the off-ramp flag, and 'we're broader' is a differentiation argument editors have heard die a thousand times. This is the highest preemption-risk concept on the table; the file itself calls it the fastest-closing window, and unannounced GLP-1 deals are stacked at every Big 5 right now. I'd sell it, but only at sprint speed and only with the MD attached before the proposal circulates.

[marketer] The media beat is live and I don't have to create it — NPR, CNBC, WaPo are covering coverage-collapse weekly, and open enrollment is a guaranteed annual detonation, which is exactly the regenerating peg structure I want. The buyer was spending $1,200/month, so price resistance is zero, and the insurance-combat chapter books consumer-advocacy segments no diet book can touch. But originality is only moderate: BenBella already planted a flag on the off-ramp and Penguin has a platformed MD one greenlight away from a maintenance sequel — this window is genuinely the fastest-closing, and the honest-regain message is a publicist's hardest sell even though it's the right book.
kill_risks: [editor] Author dependency: without a credentialed, media-fluent clinician signed at proposal stage, this collapses into either an alarmist journalist book or an unsellable academic one — and the named acquisition targets are a wish list, not a shortlist.
[agent] Diet buyers buy hope, not aftermath — and Penguin can greenlight a Salas-Whalen maintenance sequel with a built-in platform in one phone call, which vaporizes the auction before it starts.

[marketer] Aftermath books fight purchase psychology: diet buyers buy hope, and 'most of it comes back — defend your 25%' is honest copy that loses the thumbnail war to any rival willing to overpromise.

score_table.title: So You Bought a Bible
rationales: [editor] The safest P&L in the set: 63M caregivers, the strongest buying demographic, a documented $10K/year spend, a proven 500K-copy category, and a multi-copy-per-family sales structure. Authors are abundant (care managers, discharge planners, eldercare creators), the content is fully documented, and the demographics are ironclad through 2040, never mind 2028. What it lacks is any real originality — crisis-sequencing an existing category bible is a format play, not a new book — and 'Peak 80' is a manufactured peg on an evergreen shelf, which is why urgency scores modestly. But evergreen-with-a-format-edge is exactly what backlists, and I would publish this happily as a reliable compounder rather than a lead title.

[agent] The triage-not-encyclopedia inversion is genuinely smart structural differentiation, and the author bench (care managers, eldercare creators, well-desk journalists) is the deepest in the field — this book definitely gets written and written well. The sibling multi-buy and institutional channels are real P&L helpers. But 'Peak 80' is a soft peg dressed as a hard one; eldercare has been 'about to explode' for twenty years and remains a backlist grind, not an auction category. Being Mortal sold on mortality and Gawande, not on caregiving logistics. Solid acquisition at a sane advance; nobody preempts it, which tells you something.

[marketer] The most intense, most self-aware pain in the field — parents watching a dated deadline approach are the definition of a motivated buyer — and the marketing plan is the cheapest here because the channel already exists: parent centers, Arc chapters, and transition fairs are standing distribution I don't have to buy. The ABLE-Act 'every book in print is now wrong' hook is a legitimately great trade-ad line, though a regulatory peg is a feature story, not a fear cycle. Buildability is the best in the set (stable rules, cheap author search, org endorsements over follower counts). I rank it third precisely because it's the honest 36-Hour-Day play: guaranteed compounding backlist beats two of the speculative-heat concepts, but it will never lead a list and shouldn't be scored as if it could.
kill_risks: [editor] Discoverability against a 500K-copy incumbent: the buyer searches mid-crisis on Amazon, Morris still owns the results page, and a Workman 4th edition announcement mid-cycle turns our '2014 is stale' jacket claim into a knife fight we lose on brand equity.

[agent] Eldercare is a category where the demographics never convert: buyers Google in the ambulance bay instead of buying books, and this must out-discover a 500K-copy incumbent in a shelf lane publishers quietly know underperforms its market size.

[marketer] Ceiling: first-year velocity is too low for trade frontlist economics, and the free nonprofit PDFs that make the best sales channel are also the free substitute — the book risks becoming a workshop handout with an ISBN.

score_table.title: When the School Bus Stops Coming
rationales: [editor] Hot today, and that is precisely the problem — this is the concept my 18-month staleness allergy was built for. The forced-off wave is real and the pain is acute, but the pharma pipeline is the fastest-moving variable in publishing right now, and 'most of it comes back, defend your 25%' is the most honest and least sellable promise in this field; the overpromising fast-follower outsells us in year one. BenBella already planted the off-ramp flag, Penguin has a bestselling author one maintenance-sequel away, and the concept's own open_questions concede the window may already be shut. High buildability, competent architecture, wrong moment to pay a lead-title advance.

[agent] The sharpest pain in the entire field — these parents are terrified, self-aware, and already paying attorneys — and the ABLE Act regulatory hook that makes every competing title 'checkably wrong' is the best why-now mechanism here because it expires the competition rather than outshouting it. Zero preemption risk, cheap author search, honest open_questions. But I get paid on advances and this is a $75K deal that compounds quietly for fifteen years, not an auction. The 36-Hour Day model is real; it's just a university-press or Workman business, and the panel should score it as the best book and the smallest deal in the set.

[marketer] The Thanksgiving duty-purchase structure and the bank/credit-union bulk channel are exactly what I look for, and the news pegs regenerate reliably (IC3 every spring, each celebrity deepfake). But this concept has a fatal marketing self-contradiction the pitch treats as an asset: 'safe word, hang up, call back, wait 24 hours' fits on a wallet card, and CBS is already giving the wallet card away. The kink connotation on the title is not a coin flip — it's a disqualifier for a gift book bought by 55-year-old women for their mothers, and the tested alternates concede the point. Vendor-sourced stats (1,210%, 1,600%) will shrink under fact-check and take the jacket copy with them.
kill_risks: [editor] The 2028 landscape dissolves the premise: cheap oral GLP-1s and price competition shrink the forced-off population between deal and pub date, leaving a book about a 2026 insurance crisis on a 2028 shelf — while diet buyers keep proving they purchase hope, not aftermath.
[agent] The ceiling: a trade-paperback bulk-channel franchise that tops out at numbers no Big-5 P&L gets excited about, in a category where the nonprofit sales force also gives the content away free.

[marketer] The entire protocol is teachable in a free 90-second TV segment or AARP web page — the demand signal (safe-word meme everywhere) is also the substitute — and the title reads BDSM at Amazon-thumbnail distance, which poisons the exact 'hand it to Mom at Thanksgiving' gift purchase the model depends on.

score_table.title: The Safe Word
rationales: [editor] The most original concept on the table — the fluency-not-faith move genuinely exploits a structural seam neither Christian houses nor secular imprints can occupy, and if it works it is a 30-year backlist annuity in the Mere Christianity slot. But pain is the lowest in the field (curiosity is not a 2 a.m. purchase), the buyer's need is diffuse, and the author search is a 6-12 month unicorn hunt where a miss produces exactly the apologetics book the concept exists to avoid. The units-sold data is the hardest evidence in the set and the trend reads durable into 2028, so I keep this warm — but I acquire it only after the author exists, not before.

[agent] The 'named protocol' flag-plant is the right strategy and the Gift of Fear architecture answers the dating risk honestly. But I've sold into this category: scam-defense books are reliable midlist, never breakout — Abagnale with maximum celebrity moved modest numbers, and this package has no celebrity. The vendor-sourced statistics (Vectra's 1,210%) will get shredded in a serious vetting pass, the title's kink problem is worse than the open_questions concedes because it kills the exact gift-purchase mechanic the whole plan depends on, and AARP fast-tracking its own house title is a live threat, not a hypothetical. Buildable, timely, and commercially capped.

[marketer] The pain is acute and the sibling multi-buy ('read chapter 8 before Sunday') is a genuinely clever conversion structure, plus eldercare TikTok gives me a real creator channel and the chapter titles excerpt natively. But 'Peak 80' is the weakest why-now in the field — an evergreen category dressed up as an event — and originality is format-deep only: Workman can commission a 4th edition, Hachette already has the millennial-caregiver headline, and my publicity pitches will collide with Generation Care in every producer's inbox. Solid, buildable, but it's the concept most likely to be outspent rather than outflanked.
kill_risks: [editor] The conversion assumption: 19M Bible purchases are heavily aesthetic/identity-driven (premium bindings, journaling Bibles), and there is zero direct evidence those buyers purchase a companion explainer — the whole P&L rests on an inference.

[agent] The free-content substitution problem is fatal at the register: AARP and NCOA give away 90% of the protocol, and a duty/fear purchase satisfied by a web guide never converts to a $28 hardcover at scale.

[marketer] Point-of-need discoverability: the buyer searches mid-crisis and Amazon/Google serve them the 500K-copy Morris incumbent — a format advantage is invisible in a search-results thumbnail, so winning requires paid spend the category's margins won't fund.

score_table.title: The Parent Emergency
rationales: [editor] The most acute, most self-aware pain in the entire field — these families already pay attorneys and conference fees, the ABLE regulatory peg genuinely obsoletes the shelf, and the demographic wave runs a decade. Buildability is strong because org trust beats platform in this category, making the author search the cheapest here. But I am a Big-5 editor and this is not a Big-5 book: 50K families a year is a niche annuity, the free-PDF nonprofit channel is both the sales force and the competition, and no rep walks into B&N with a lead-title pitch for it. Someone should publish this; it just should not be me at hardcover-advance money.

[agent] The freshest thesis on the table — 'fluency, not faith' is a structural move no incumbent can copy, and religion is one of the only growth categories in print, something most panels don't know. But pain is the lowest here: curiosity is not crisis, and curiosity buyers browse rather than need. The undisclosed weakness the proposal soft-pedals: a large share of that 19M-unit figure is premium-binding and replacement purchasing by existing believers — the Gen-Z-seeker share is publisher spin on top of Circana data, and the 'buys a Bible, buys the companion' conversion is pure hypothesis. Highest variance in the field: a once-a-decade backlist annuity if the unicorn author materializes, and nothing at all if she doesn't — and the 6-12 month critical path means I can't even take it out this cycle.

[marketer] Best title in the field — it wins the 3-second thumbnail test outright and claims the exact purchase moment — and the fluency-not-faith frame is genuinely original. But curiosity is not pain; nobody buys this at 2 a.m. in a panic, and the annual 'why is Gen Z buying Bibles' news cycle is a soft feature peg, not a fear engine. The gift channel is real (the Strobel engine) but the seam positioning is a marketing-copy tightrope with hostile readers on both flanks, and the 6-12 month author search sits directly on the critical path. I'd rather see this as a nimble mid-size acquisition than a lead title.
kill_risks: [editor] The ceiling: this is a Johns Hopkins/Workman-model decade-long backlist reference with bulk-channel economics, and a Big-5 hardcover P&L built on frontlist velocity simply does not clear — the concept admits it, and the concept is right.

[agent] The author search fails or lands wrong: without a genuinely credible translator-not-evangelist under 40, the seam positioning collapses and you get an apologetics book Christian retail won't need and secular media won't trust.

[marketer] The conversion assumption fails at retail: 19M Bible purchases never translate into explainer purchases, and the seam positioning means neither B&N nor Christian retail merchandises it — a book both channels can stock is often a book neither champions.

judge_verdicts.verdict: I would acquire The Safe Word — retitled — because it is the only concept where every axis a P&L actually runs on points the same direction: the threat provably grows through 2028 rather than risking staleness, the author package is findable in a quarter rather than a unicorn hunt, the rep one-liner ('The Gift of Fear for the deepfake era') sells itself to a B&N buyer in one breath, and the dual-generation duty-gift purchase plus bank/advisor bulk channel gives it de Becker-style backlist legs. The Perfect Listener has a higher ceiling and I would chase it hard — but only with a Damour-class clinician already signed, because concept-first proposals with single-survey evidence bases are how editors get fired. Everything else is either a timing bet against the pharma pipeline (Off-Ramp), an author search disguised as a proposal (Bible), or a fine book for somebody else's imprint (School Bus).

I acquire The Perfect Listener, and I take it out within sixty days. It is the only concept in the field where a proven 2M-copy buyer demographic, a verifiably empty shelf, a stacked and citable why-now, and ownable artifacts (the scripts, the Family AI Agreement, the Breakup protocol) converge — that combination is what produces a six-house auction rather than a respectable sale. The author dependency is real but solvable inside one proposal cycle because the concept names an actual bench (Stanford Brainstorm, Child Mind, Common Sense advisors) rather than wishing for a unicorn, which is exactly what sinks the Bible concept. The Off-Ramp is the runner-up on raw audience size, but its window is closing fastest and its 'honest regain' message fights the category's hope-buying psychology; I'd co-agent it only with the MD already signed. Everything else is either a midlist grind, a capped backlist annuity, or a thesis without a throat to speak it.

I acquire The Perfect Listener. It is the only concept in the field where the marketing engine is fully load-bearing before a word is written: a morning-show-ready stat (33% chose the bot over a human), a downloadable viral artifact with a proven distribution precedent, regenerating legal and survey pegs on a calendar, a counterprogramming hook ('don't ban the AI girlfriend') that differentiates every booking, and the Haidt-proven PTA bulk channel waiting for the sequel. The Off-Ramp is the close second and I would chase it too, but its category requires selling honesty against rivals selling hope, and its window is visibly closing. The Perfect Listener's one real vulnerability — author dependency — is a solvable search problem with named acquisition paths; every other concept's kill risk is structural to the market itself. Move at proposal speed and lock the clinician before Labor Day.

concept.subtitle: What to Do When Your Kid's Closest Relationship Is With an AI — Scripts, House Rules, and a Way Back to You
concept.one_line_pitch: The Anxious Generation told parents the phone stole their kid's childhood; The Perfect Listener is the first book for the parents who just discovered the phone now loves their kid back — a clinician's protocol book, with word-for-word scripts, for detecting, defusing, and out-relating a teen's attachment to an AI companion, for the 72% of American teens already using one.
concept.category: Family & Relationships / Parenting — Teenagers (FAM039000); hardcover prescriptive nonfiction with reported case-study spine, à la Anxious Generation / Good Inside
concept.target_reader: Primary: mothers 35-55 of kids 10-17 — the exact demographic that kept The Anxious Generation at #1 NYT for over a year. The addressable base is parents of the ~42M US kids aged 8-17; per Common Sense Media's national survey (n=1,060, NORC), 72% of teens have used AI companions, 52% use them regularly, and 33% have chosen an AI over a human for a serious conversation — meaning tens of millions of households already have the problem and most parents don't know it (parental unawareness documented by Child Mind Institute and Children and Screens). Secondary: school counselors, therapists, teachers, and PTAs — a schools/bulk-buy institutional channel the Haidt playbook already proved out.
concept.core_promise: In one weekend of reading, a frightened parent goes from "I just found the chat logs and I have no idea what this is" to a working plan: how to recognize the six signs of companion attachment, how to open the first conversation without triggering shame or secrecy (word-for-word scripts, with the failure modes annotated), how to set age-tiered house rules via a one-page Family AI Agreement, when a chatbot relationship is benign and when it's a crisis (sexual content, self-harm reinforcement), how to run a "breakup" that doesn't backfire — and how to rebuild the human connection the bot was substituting for. The reframe that carries the book: this is not a screen-time problem, it's a relationship in your kid's life, and you handle it the way you'd handle any relationship you're worried about — you can't ban it, but you can out-relate it.
concept.why_now: The catalysts are stacked, dated, and citable from the research record: Common Sense Media's "unacceptable risk" verdict (July 2025); the Stanford risk assessment showing companions producing sexual content and self-harm encouragement with teen personas (Aug 2025); Character.AI banning under-18 open chat (Nov 2025) — which pushed minors toward unmoderated platforms rather than ending use; the Character.AI/Google teen-suicide settlements (Jan 2026); California SB 243 in force since Jan 1, 2026, with enforcement stories now regenerating coverage; and MIT Technology Review naming AI companions a 2026 Breakthrough Technology. An arXiv study documents teens describing their own use in clinical addiction language ("withdrawal, tolerance, relapse"). Meanwhile the demand signal is the stopgap flood itself: APA, Child Mind, PolitiFact, KQED, UNICEF, NCOA-style institutional web guides are all filling a book-shaped hole — and a July 2026 preemption check confirms no mainstream-publisher trade book has been announced. Every prior "kids and tech" wave (social media → Haidt) produced its definitive parent book 2-3 years after the panic began; for AI companions that window is exactly now.

toc.summary: Three reported family stories — discovery of the logs, the confrontation that went wrong, the quiet kid nobody suspected — anchored by the national numbers (72% have used companions, 52% regularly, 33% choosing the bot over a human for serious conversations). Thesis: this is not social media 2.0; it's the first technology that performs love.

Why the bot wins: frictionless intimacy, engineered sycophancy, infinite availability, zero judgment — a product built to be the perfect listener your household can't be. What the design tells us about which kids are most vulnerable and why lectures about 'it's not real' fail on contact.

toc.chapter: 3. It's a Relationship, Not Screen Time
toc.summary: The book's governing reframe. Teens describe their own use in withdrawal/tolerance/relapse language; treating a relationship like a device quota produces secrecy, not safety. Character.AI's under-18 ban as the case study: prohibition moved kids to darker platforms. The alternative model: parent it like a relationship you're worried about.

Detection without betrayal: the six behavioral signs of companion attachment, which apps and companion modes to know (and how they hide), what the platforms log, and how to look in a way you can later defend to your kid. Includes the Attachment Check-In — a 10-question self-assessment for parents.

The heart of the book: full scripts for opening the topic — the curious open, the discovered-logs conversation, the 'my friend's kid' sideways entry — with the five sentences that slam the door ('It's not real,' 'That's pathetic') annotated as failure modes. How to Talk So Kids Will Listen, rebuilt for synthetic love.

A parent's field guide to the companion landscape — dedicated apps, companion modes inside mainstream chatbots and social platforms, age gates and their workarounds, what SB 243 and the post-settlement rules actually require. Organized by attachment dynamics, not brand names, so it doesn't expire with the next app cycle.

The Family AI Agreement: age-tiered rules (8-12, 13-15, 16-18) covering what AI talk is fine, what's private-but-discussed, what's off-limits — negotiated with the kid, not imposed. Includes the one-page agreement itself, the book's shareable artifact.

When it's a crisis: sexual content with minors, self-harm encouragement, and suicidal ideation surfacing in bot chats — grounded in the Stanford risk-assessment findings and the litigation record. A concrete escalation protocol: what to screenshot, whom to call, when a therapist is non-negotiable, what to demand from the platform.

Ending an attachment without driving it underground: harm-reduction taper versus cold turkey, anticipating real grief (kids mourn these relationships), managing relapse, and the parent's script for the day the bot gets deleted. The chapter no web guide has dared to write.

The upstream chapter: the loneliness, social anxiety, or family friction the bot was medicating. Practical rebuild moves — engineered low-stakes peer contact, the 10-minute nightly no-agenda conversation, becoming askable — with the honest admission that the bot listened better than most adults do, and what to copy from it.

The 8-12s, where companions are colonizing the imaginary-friend years; neurodivergent kids, for whom bots can be both genuine practice ground and deeper trap; and the teen who insists, with evidence, that the AI helps — how to take that seriously without surrendering.

The credibility chapter: benign and even beneficial use exists, and the research says so. A calibrated green/yellow/red framework so readers trust the alarms in chapters 8-9 — and so the book survives expert scrutiny that a moral-panic treatment wouldn't.

The collective-action layer that made Haidt a movement: what to ask your school to do, what SB 243 and the settlements entitle you to demand of platforms, model language for PTA policies, and where the regulatory fight goes next.

The long game: companions are a 2026 Breakthrough Technology and they are not going away; the goal is not an AI-free child but a child for whom synthetic intimacy is a tool, not a home. Closes the loop on the three families from chapter 1.

comps.title: The Anxious Generation
comps.author: Jonathan Haidt
comps.relevance: Proves the buyer and the playbook — #1 NYT for over a year, 2M+ copies, sold to this exact parent demographic with a diagnosis-plus-collective-action structure. But it predates companion bots and covers phones/social media; The Perfect Listener is the next book its readers need, not a competitor.

comps.title: How to Talk So Kids Will Listen & Listen So Kids Will Talk
comps.author: Adele Faber & Elaine Mazlish
comps.relevance: The scripts DNA and proof of format: parents pay, for decades, for word-for-word dialogue guidance. This book applies that mechanism to a subject Faber/Mazlish could never have imagined — the conversation about a synthetic rival.

comps.title: Good Inside
comps.author: Becky Kennedy
comps.relevance: Proves a clinician with scripts and a connection-over-control philosophy can hit #1 NYT in the current market — the tonal and authorial model for this book (warm, non-shaming, protocol-driven).

comps.title: Raising AI: An Essential Guide to Parenting Our Future
comps.author: De Kai
comps.relevance: Proves publisher and media appetite at the AI-parenting intersection — and defines the gap by what it isn't: a philosophy of humanity 'parenting' AI, explicitly not a how-to for a parent holding their kid's chat logs. Its existence de-risks the category; its framing leaves the practical lane empty.

comps.title: The Emotional Lives of Teenagers
comps.author: Lisa Damour
comps.relevance: Recent proof that a credentialed adolescent psychologist writing directly to worried parents about teen emotional life is an instant NYT bestseller — the platform template for this book's ideal author.

concept.differentiation: The shelf is verifiably empty and every occupant is disqualified by design: Haidt (2024) predates companion bots and his own 2026 follow-on output stays in the phones/social-media lane; De Kai's Raising AI is MIT Press philosophy, not a how-to; and three independent research scouts found nothing else but KDP spam (listings with 'BLOCKBUSTER STORY' and 'KITS FOR LIFE' as author names) — a July 2026 search confirms the only credible resources remain institutional web guides, which are the demand signal, not the competition. Against the risk notes directly: (1) Preemption by a litigation-beat journalist — the journalist's book is a diagnosis/exposé (what happened at Character.AI); this is a protocol book (what do I do Tuesday night), a different shelf that survives and even benefits from an exposé landing first, the way Good Inside coexists with The Anxious Generation. (2) Preemption by Haidt or a Common Sense-affiliated author — Haidt's lane is occupied by his own phone thesis; a Haidt companion-bot chapter cannot deliver scripts, tiered house rules, a breakup protocol, and a Family AI Agreement, which are this book's ownable artifacts. (3) Speed — the evidence base (Common Sense/NORC survey, Stanford risk assessment, arXiv addiction-language study, settlement record, SB 243) is already public and stable; a proposal needs no new fieldwork and the book is writable in 9 months, and the concept's moat is the counterintuitive thesis a fast-follower will flinch from: you can't ban a relationship — bans demonstrably backfire (the Nov 2025 Character.AI under-18 ban), so you have to out-relate the machine. (4) Platform churn dating the book — chapters organize around attachment dynamics and protocols, not app taxonomy (the field-guide chapter is explicitly brand-agnostic), the same evergreen architecture that keeps Faber/Mazlish selling after 45 years.
concept.author_profile: Ideal: a clinical psychologist or adolescent psychiatrist with an active teen practice and demonstrated media fluency — the Lisa Damour/Becky Kennedy archetype — optionally paired with a tech-accountability journalist for the reported spine. Credibility requirements: clinical license (the Red Zone and Breakup chapters demand it), fluency in the actual research (Common Sense, Stanford, the arXiv literature), and no moral-panic track record. Realistic acquisition paths: the clinician-researchers behind Stanford's Aug 2025 companion risk assessment (Stanford Brainstorm orbit); Common Sense Media's research leads or advisors on the Talk, Trust, and Trade-Offs survey; Child Mind Institute clinicians already writing its AI-chatbot parent guidance; APA task-force members on teens and chatbots; or expert witnesses from the Character.AI litigation — each already holds the evidence base, a platform, and institutional endorsement channels (Common Sense, APA, Child Mind) that convert directly into launch media and school bulk sales.
marketing_hooks: Regenerating news pegs on a schedule: SB 243 enforcement actions through 2026-27, the Jan 2027 settlement anniversary, each new state copycat bill, and every Common Sense survey update re-opens the segment — and the author is the bookable expert each time

The Family AI Agreement as a viral artifact: a free, downloadable one-page household contract, engineered to spread through school newsletters and parent group chats the way Wait Until 8th and Haidt's phone-free pledge did — every download is a lead for the book

Morning-show-ready diagnostic: 'Six signs your kid has an AI best friend' plus the 72%/52%/33% stats is a complete GMA/Today segment in a sentence; the 33%-choose-the-bot-over-you stat is the single most shareable number in parenting media right now

Institutional bulk channel: PTAs, school counselors, and districts already buying Anxious Generation class sets need the companion-bot sequel for back-to-school 2027; Common Sense Media and Child Mind partnerships (both currently publishing stopgap web guides) convert to endorsements and distribution

Parent-confessional virality: 'I read my daughter's AI chat logs' first-person content is already a proven TikTok/Substack genre; the book supplies the vocabulary (companion attachment, the Quiet Audit, the Breakup) that creators will adopt and cite

Counterprogramming hook for earned media: 'The parenting expert who says DON'T ban your kid's AI girlfriend' — the anti-ban thesis is built for op-ed pages and podcast debate bookings, differentiating every interview from generic AI-panic coverage
concept.open_questions: Honest weaknesses a skeptic should raise: (1) Pipeline invisibility — unannounced Big-5 deals can't be searched; my July 2026 check found no announced competitor, but a proposal from a Character.AI-beat journalist could surface any month, and the practical-scripts moat only holds if the author and publisher move at proposal speed. (2) Article-sufficiency risk — the free APA/Child Mind/Common Sense guides may satisfy most parents' anxiety at $0; the bet is that parents in active crisis (discovered logs, red-zone content) trade up to book-length help, which is analogous to but not proven by the Anxious Generation precedent. (3) Evidence-base concentration — the headline 72%/52%/33% numbers all trace to one Common Sense/NORC survey (n=1,060, self-report); if replication softens them, the urgency case weakens, and the book must be written to survive that. (4) Platform volatility cuts both ways — protocols-not-products framing mitigates dating, but a major industry retreat from under-18 companions (post-settlement) could shrink the panic before pub date, though the Character.AI ban evidence suggests use migrates rather than stops. (5) Author dependency — this is a concept-first proposal; without a credentialed, media-fluent clinician it collapses into either an alarmist journalist book or an unsellable academic one, and the named acquisition paths are plausible but uncontracted. (6) Moral-panic backlash — researchers (including the CHI 2026 literature) document benefits alongside harms; if the book overclaims, the expert class that made Haidt controversial will make this book radioactive — hence chapter 12 is load-bearing, not a hedge.

concept.subtitle: Life After Ozempic — How to Keep the Weight Off, Quiet the Food Noise, and Decide Whether to Go Back
concept.one_line_pitch: Thirty million Americans took the most effective weight-loss drug in history, and now insurers are throwing them off it — this is the first major book for the after: a doctor-and-journalist guide to the taper, the regain science, the returning food noise, the 'Ozempic personality' whiplash, and the restart decision, written for people who were never given an exit plan. A physician in April 2026 coverage said it outright: 'We don't have a good off-ramp right now.' This book is the off-ramp.
concept.category: Health & Fitness / Weight Loss (HEA019000), with Psychology/Self-Help crossover — hardcover prescriptive nonfiction, 80-90K words, lead title positioning
concept.target_reader: PRIMARY: the forced-off GLP-1 user — skewing women 50-64, the core health/self-help buying demographic. Scale from the research file: 1 in 8 US adults currently take a GLP-1 and 18% have ever taken one (KFF) — 30M+ people; 41M commercially insured Americans now have zero Wegovy coverage (+42% YoY) and 109M lost Zepbound coverage (GoodRx via NPR, June 2026). These buyers were spending $900-1,400/month on this exact problem, so a $30 hardcover is rounding error. SECONDARY: current users pre-planning their exit (every user knows the cliff is coming — that anxiety is a purchase trigger while still on the drug), partners/spouses, and the dietitians, therapists, and primary-care clinicians improvising discontinuation care with no manual.
concept.core_promise: You are not a willpower failure with an expiring prescription — you are a patient abandoned mid-treatment, and there is a navigable path through it. The reader gets: a medically grounded taper-vs-stop decision; a maintenance protocol built on what the regain science actually shows (Cambridge, Jan 2026: most weight returns within ~18 months, but ~25% of losses are typically kept — the book teaches you to defend and extend that 25%); tools for the returning food noise and the psychological aftermath ('Ozempic personality' reversal, body image, reshaped relationships); a cost-triage map when paying cash, and an honest, criteria-based framework for the restart decision — including how to live as a cycler if on-off-on becomes your permanent reality.
concept.why_now: The forced-discontinuation wave is peaking on a citable schedule. Coverage collapse is happening in real time: BCBS of Massachusetts and Michigan, Harvard Pilgrim, HCA, and North Carolina Medicaid all dropped GLP-1 coverage effective 2026 (NPR, June 23, 2026), and CNBC confirmed on July 8, 2026 — yesterday — that employers are not expanding coverage. GoodRx data via NPR: 41M people now have zero Wegovy coverage (+42% YoY); 109M lost Zepbound coverage. Simultaneously, the science of the aftermath just arrived: the January 2026 Cambridge analysis quantified the regain curve (most weight back within ~18 months, ~25% kept), the Washington Post covered regain the same week, and a PMC meta-analysis anchors the medical spine. The first mass user cohorts (2023-24 starters) are hitting maintenance or forced exit at the same moment — a demographic pulse, not a news blip. And every autumn open-enrollment season re-detonates the story for years of publicity cycles.

toc.chapter: 1. Kicked Off: The Great GLP-1 Exile
toc.summary: The reported spine: patients learning at the pharmacy counter that the drug that changed their life is now $1,200/month. The coverage-collapse map (BCBS MA/MI, Harvard Pilgrim, HCA, NC Medicaid, the employer retreat) and why this is a mid-treatment abandonment crisis, not a lifestyle story.

toc.chapter: 2. What the Drug Actually Did to You
toc.summary: The mechanism chapter, written for the exit: appetite circuitry, 'food noise,' metabolic adaptation — why what happens after you stop is biology, not moral failure. Sets up the book's central reframe: obesity is chronic, so discontinuation is a medical event that deserves a protocol.

An unflinching tour of the regain evidence — the Cambridge analysis, the meta-analytic regain curves — and the number nobody markets: roughly a quarter of losses are typically kept. The book's honest thesis: your job is to defend and widen that 25%, and here is how.

Cold stop, step-down dosing, dose-stretching: what evidence and clinical practice actually support, what is improvisation, and how to build an exit plan with (or despite) a prescriber who was never trained for this conversation.

The physiological to-do list for the off-ramp: muscle preservation, protein architecture, strength training as non-negotiable, sleep, and the handful of habits with real evidence for holding weight without the drug.

toc.chapter: 6. When the Food Noise Comes Back
toc.summary: The returning internal chatter is the most feared symptom of stopping. Behavioral and clinical tools — from hunger-mapping to urge-surfing to medication-adjacent alternatives — for managing appetite that is louder than your willpower was ever meant to handle.

Users reported flattened cravings, changed drinking, altered mood on the drug — and a documented identity whiplash coming off ('Ozempic personality,' Healthline/Calm explainer wave). Who are you when the volume knob turns back up? The psychological aftermath the clinical conversation misses.

Body image after rapid loss and partial regain: dysmorphia, loose skin, the terror of watching the scale climb, and why regain shame is clinically dangerous — drawing on the therapist-flagged eating-disorder edge of the GLP-1 era.

toc.chapter: 9. Everyone Noticed: Food, Love, and Other People
toc.summary: The drug reshaped marriages, friendships, and food-centered social lives; stopping reshapes them again. Scripts for partners, comment-deflection, and renegotiating a shared table when your appetite — and body — are changing course visibly.

For those who want to stay on: the honest money map — cash-pay programs, oral formulations, patient assistance, compounding's legal gray zone, and a clear-eyed warning tour of the counterfeit and gray-market supply chain.

The insurance playbook: appeals that work, the magic words in a prior authorization, employer-benefits advocacy, and documenting medical necessity — turning the book's villain into a chapter of actionable leverage.

A criteria-based framework — weight trajectory, metabolic markers, mental health, money — for deciding whether and when to go back on, replacing shame ('I failed off the drug') with the chronic-disease logic every other relapsing condition already gets.

The chapter no one else has written: millions will live on-and-off GLP-1s for decades as coverage, prices, and formulations churn. How to cycle deliberately — what to protect during off-phases, what to rebuild during on-phases — whatever drug or price era comes next.

Identity beyond the drug: what stability looks like when weight is managed rather than cured, and a closing argument for treating yourself as a patient with a chronic condition in a system that keeps pretending otherwise.

comps.title: Weightless: A Doctor's Guide to GLP-1 Medications, Sustainable Weight Loss, and the Health You Deserve
comps.author: Rocio Salas-Whalen, MD
comps.relevance: Proves the category is national-bestseller hot with a credentialed-MD author and even a Weight Watchers book-club pickup — but it is the on-ramp book (how to use GLP-1s). The Off-Ramp is its structural sequel for a different moment in the same reader's life; verified via PRH and Amazon listings.

comps.title: The Ozempic Revolution
comps.author: Alexandra Sowa, MD
comps.relevance: The current category leader; reviewers in the research file call it beginner-level and on-ramp focused. Demonstrates Big-5 appetite and price point while leaving the discontinuation reader completely unserved.

comps.title: Losing the Weight Loss Meds: A 10-Week Playbook for Stopping GLP-1 Medications Without Regaining the Weight
comps.author: Holly R. Wyatt, MD & James O. Hill, PhD
comps.relevance: The closest direct comp and the proof-of-demand canary: a mid-size press moved first on the off-ramp. Verified: it is a narrow 10-week transition program — per the research file's coverage it omits identity, relationships, body image, cost triage, insurance combat, and the restart/cycling decision, which is most of this book.

comps.title: Magic Pill: The Extraordinary Benefits and Disturbing Risks of the New Weight-Loss Drugs
comps.author: Johann Hari
comps.relevance: Proves a mainstream general-trade audience buys GLP-1 books at bestseller scale beyond the diet shelf — but it is diagnosis and debate, not a manual. The Off-Ramp converts that proven curiosity into prescriptive help.

comps.title: Quit Like a Woman
comps.author: Holly Whitaker
comps.relevance: The voice-and-frame comp: a quitting-a-substance book that fused protocol with identity and cultural critique and became a durable backlist bestseller for the same female 35-64 buyer. Signals to editors that 'stopping a drug' can be an identity book, not just a program.

concept.differentiation: Taking the file's three risks head-on. (1) FASTEST-CLOSING WINDOW (BenBella's Wyatt book, Penguin's Weightless): the shelf is split into two half-books, and this concept owns the whole. Weightless and The Ozempic Revolution are on-ramps; Wyatt/Hill is a 10-week quit program that — per the research file's own coverage — omits identity, relationships, body image, the gray market, cost triage, and the restart decision. No title on the shelf is written for the FORCED-off patient (the 41M/109M coverage-loss population), none treats insurance combat as content (Ch. 11 is a weapon no diet book has), and none concedes the honest Cambridge math (most regain, ~25% kept) and builds the program around defending that number instead of promising exceptionalism. A fast-follower can copy a taper plan; they cannot easily copy the merged medical+psychological+financial architecture, which the research file itself flags as 'more original and defensible than either scout's half alone.' Speed matters, so this proposal is built to go out in weeks, with the July 8, 2026 CNBC employer data as page one. (2) CREDENTIALED-AUTHOR REQUIREMENT: baked into the package as an obesity-medicine MD + named health journalist pair — see author_profile. (3) DRUG-LANDSCAPE CHURN (oral GLP-1s, price cuts could soften the forced-off dynamic): the book is deliberately drug-agnostic and decision-framework-shaped. Chapters 12-13 reframe the entire premise around cycling — on-and-off as the permanent condition of GLP-1 life under ANY price regime — so cheaper orals don't obsolete the book; they just change which chapter the reader starts with. Regain biology, food-noise psychology, and maintenance physiology are evergreen regardless of what the drugs cost in 2028.
concept.author_profile: Non-negotiable per the research file: a credentialed medical author. Optimal package is an obesity-medicine MD with a discontinuation-heavy patient panel PAIRED with a national health journalist (the Hari-proven trade voice plus clinical authority Wyatt/Hill lacked in trade packaging). Realistic paths: (a) an academic obesity physician from the STEP/SURMOUNT trial ecosystem or a university weight-management center who is watching patients lose coverage weekly and wants the definitive book; (b) the chief medical officer of a major telehealth/obesity platform, who sits on unmatched discontinuation data and has an in-house comms machine; (c) an MD with an existing GLP-1-focused social platform (the Salas-Whalen path — her bestseller proves this archetype converts) co-writing with a health reporter already on the coverage-collapse beat at NPR/CNBC/WaPo, all of which are actively covering it per the file's sources. Psychology gets a credibility spine via a contributing eating-disorder clinician for chapters 7-9. The journalist co-author also de-risks speed: reported chapters can be drafted from the existing 2026 coverage record while the MD builds protocols.
marketing_hooks: Open-enrollment season is a built-in annual publicity engine: every October-November, millions discover their GLP-1 coverage is gone (BCBS MA/MI, Harvard Pilgrim, HCA, NC Medicaid already dropped for 2026; CNBC 7/8/26 confirms employers are not expanding) — time hardcover pub for early fall and ride the denial-letter news cycle every year thereafter.

The 'Ozempic personality' discourse is a proven viral vector (Healthline/Calm explainer wave documented in the research) — chapter 7 is engineered to be the excerpt that travels on TikTok/Instagram, where GLP-1 content already commands enormous engagement.

The Cambridge 18-month regain finding (Jan 2026) gives every interview a shocking, sourced statistic: 'Most of it comes back within 18 months — unless you do this.' The Washington Post, NPR, and CNBC are already covering regain and coverage loss, so the media pitch is joining a live beat, not creating one.

Institutional channel proven by comp behavior: Weight Watchers ran Weightless as a book-club pick — WW, Noom, Found, and telehealth obesity platforms need an off-boarding resource for members losing coverage, a bulk-buy channel no diet-book competitor is courting.

Clinician second market: dietitians, therapists, and primary-care doctors have no discontinuation manual (the MUSC physician quote — 'We don't have a good off-ramp right now' — is the trade-ad copy), enabling CME-adjacent promotion and professional word-of-mouth like The 36-Hour Day achieved in caregiving.

The insurance-combat chapter is earned-media catnip: 'The book that teaches you to fight your insurer' frames consumer-advocacy segments (GMA, local news, personal-finance podcasts) that pure diet books can never book.
concept.open_questions: Honest weaknesses: (1) Invisible pipeline is the big one — the research file itself calls this the fastest-closing window in the set; Penguin could greenlight a Salas-Whalen maintenance sequel with a built-in bestseller platform, and an unannounced Big-5 off-ramp title may already be under contract. Mitigation is speed and the forced-off/insurance-combat/cycler framing no announced title claims, but the risk is real and unpriceable. (2) Aspiration-vs-aftermath purchase psychology: diet buyers historically buy hope (on-ramp), and it is unproven at scale that people buy books about the hard part — Wyatt/Hill's sales trajectory (too new to read) is the live test; our hedge is that fear-of-the-cliff among CURRENT users makes this a pre-need purchase too, but that is an argument, not data. (3) The honest regain message is commercially double-edged — 'most people regain, defend your 25%' is credible but less sellable than a false promise, and a rival willing to overpromise may outsell us in year one. (4) Drug-landscape churn could genuinely soften the premise: if cheap oral GLP-1s or price cuts land in 2027, the 'forced off' population shrinks; the cycler reframe covers this editorially, but the emotional urgency of the pitch would dim. (5) Several load-bearing statistics (41M/109M coverage losses, KFF prevalence) come from search-extracted secondary reporting per the research file's own method notes and need a primary-source verification pass before the proposal goes out. (6) Title risk: 'Ozempic' in the subtitle dates the package if the brand era shifts to orals — solvable, but a naming decision the publisher must make deliberately.

concept.subtitle: Christianity from Zero, for a Generation That Never Went to Church
concept.one_line_pitch: Nineteen million Americans bought a Bible last year — a 21-year high, driven by Gen Z first-time buyers — and publishing has given them nothing to read next; this is the culturally fluent, zero-assumed-knowledge companion those buyers are already googling for: Mere Christianity for the algorithm-raised, written to translate rather than convert.
concept.category: Religion / Christianity — General (REL070000), hardcover trade nonfiction (field-guide/narrative hybrid) from a big-5 general imprint, with deliberate crossover distribution into Christian retail — the seam between the two channels is the positioning.
concept.target_reader: PRIMARY: spiritually curious adults 18-28 raised entirely secular — the cohort behind ~19M US Bible sales in 2025 (2x 2019), the one-year jump in US Bible users from 38% to 41% (millions of new adults), and ~67M religion-category units (+5.4%) in an otherwise flat print market, with Tyndale and HarperCollins publicly attributing growth to Gen Z first-time buyers. SECONDARY: (a) parents/grandparents/friends gift-buying for a curious young adult — the engine that built the Strobel and Lewis backlists; (b) institutional bulk buyers: campus ministries, Alpha-style newcomer courses, and churches stocking a non-preachy book they can safely hand a visitor. The trend is transatlantic (UK Bible sales nearly doubled 2019-2024), supporting a simultaneous UK deal.
concept.core_promise: In one book, a reader with zero religious background goes from total Christian illiteracy to genuine fluency: able to open the Bible they just bought without drowning by page 40 of Genesis, walk into any church knowing what is happening and why, understand what Christians actually believe (and where Christians disagree), hold the tradition's real crimes and scandals honestly, and decide for themselves what they think — without once being preached at. The promise is fluency, not faith; the reader keeps custody of their own conclusion.
concept.why_now: The industry itself has named the shortage: publishers report demand for 'accessible formats designed for first-time readers' outrunning supply. The purchase data is the hardest in the market-research set because it is units sold, not surveys: ~19M US Bibles in 2025 (double 2019, a 21-year high, +12%); US Bible users up 38%→41% in a single year; religion category ~67M units, +5.4%, one of the only growth categories of 2025; UK Bible sales nearly doubled 2019-2024; adjacent meaning-hunger visible in self-help +14.7%. The cultural moment is simultaneously being legitimized upmarket — Lamorna Ash's Bloomsbury 2025 book on Gen Z faith curiosity drew Telegraph/FT/New Statesman/GQ coverage — while the how-do-I-start question is being answered only by blog posts and KDP spam. Every future annual sales-data drop regenerates the 'why is Gen Z buying Bibles?' news cycle, and this book is built to be the designated answer.

toc.chapter: 1. You Are Not Alone in the Bible Aisle
toc.summary: The phenomenon, from the inside: who the 19 million buyers are, why a generation raised without religion is suddenly curious, and explicit permission to explore without deciding anything. Sets the contract: this book will make you fluent, not make you Christian.

Why every book you'd be handed at a church was written to win an argument you never joined — and why religious illiteracy, not skepticism, is the actual barrier for the secular-raised. The translation-not-persuasion method of the book.

What the Bible physically is: 66 books across a dozen genres written over a millennium, why translations differ (and which to actually use), and why no serious tradition reads Genesis as a science textbook.

The anti-Leviticus survival plan: where to start (not page one), what to skip on a first pass, how the two Testaments relate, and reading paths matched to what brought you here — grief, curiosity, beauty, or spite.
Jesus from zero: what historians across belief lines agree happened, what only faith claims, the world he lived in, and why the Roman state executed him. No stained glass, no sales pitch.

Resurrection, incarnation, Trinity: the claims Christianity cannot function without, explained with neither embarrassment nor hard sell — including what smart believers and doubters have actually said about them for two thousand years.

toc.chapter: 7. Words That Don't Mean What You Think
toc.summary: A decoder chapter: sin, grace, salvation, worship, gospel, repentance — church vocabulary translated against the therapy-speak and internet idiom the reader already owns.

toc.chapter: 8. Why There Are 45,000 Kinds of Christian
toc.summary: Denominations decoded: Catholic, Orthodox, Protestant, evangelical, mainline, Pentecostal — how they split, what each is like on a Sunday, and how to tell what a church really is from its website in ninety seconds.

toc.chapter: 9. How to Visit a Church Without Combusting
toc.summary: A pure field guide: what happens in a service minute by minute, when to stand and sit, communion etiquette for the unbaptized, how to dodge the welcome team politely, and how to leave and try a different one. The chapter designed to be photographed and shared.
Practice from zero: prayer, silence, sabbath, fasting — the low-commitment on-ramps, what practitioners say each one does, and what you're allowed to try while still unsure God exists.

Crusades, colonialism, abuse scandals, Christian nationalism: the objections taken at full strength, how honest believers reckon with them, and how to tell a healthy congregation from a dangerous one — the safety chapter no apologetics book will write.

toc.chapter: 12. Vibes, Witches, and the Meaning Crisis
toc.summary: Christianity among its real competitors for this generation — astrology, manifestation, witchtok, therapy culture, workism — and what is structurally different about a tradition with a 2,000-year memory, communal obligation, and a claim on your Sunday mornings.

Reported interviews with adult converts (and de-converts) aged 20-35: the slow, unglamorous, doubt-riddled reality versus the testimony-industrial version. Belief as a spectrum you move along, not a switch.

toc.chapter: 14. You're Allowed to Leave. You're Allowed to Stay.
toc.summary: Do you have to believe all of it? Living with doubt, choosing a level of commitment, what to read and do next in either direction — the door explicitly left open both ways, which is the whole credibility of the book.

comps.title: Mere Christianity
comps.author: C.S. Lewis
comps.relevance: The perennial on-ramp and the book still handed to every seeker; proves permanent backlist demand for a Christianity explainer. But it assumes a reader with mid-century Christian cultural memory — church weddings, school prayers, hymn literacy — that the never-churched 2026 reader simply does not have. This book replaces the assumed context, not the ambition.

comps.title: The Reason for God
comps.author: Timothy Keller
comps.relevance: NYT bestseller proving big-trade commercial appetite for intelligent, non-schlocky Christianity. Built, however, to rebut New Atheist skeptics — a debate today's 22-year-old never attended. The target reader's objection is not 'I don't believe it' but 'I don't understand it,' and no comp on the shelf serves that reader.

comps.title: The Case for Christ
comps.author: Lee Strobel
comps.relevance: 5M+ copies sold; establishes the seeker category's commercial ceiling and the gift-to-a-curious-friend purchase engine this book inherits. Its courtroom-apologetics format is now generationally alien, and its evangelistic intent is exactly what secular-raised readers report bouncing off.

comps.title: Don't Forget We're Here Forever: A New Generation's Search for Religion
comps.author: Lamorna Ash
comps.relevance: Freshest proof that mainstream literary publishing and mainstream media (Telegraph, FT, New Statesman, GQ) will champion the Gen-Z-faith-curiosity subject right now. It is memoir/reportage that documents the hunger but hands the reader no map — it creates the audience this book equips.

comps.title: The Surprising Rebirth of Belief in God
comps.author: Justin Brierley
comps.relevance: Documents the intellectual re-opening to Christianity and proves the why-now — but is published by a Christian house, addressed to Christians watching the trend, not to the curious outsider living it. Demonstrates the lane a general-imprint book for the seeker herself remains empty.

concept.differentiation: Every credible incumbent is apologetics: literature written to win an argument, from presses whose channel demands a conversion payoff. This book makes one structural move none of them can copy: it sells fluency instead of faith, keeping custody of the conclusion with the reader. That move directly exploits why the lane has stayed open — the research's own finding that big-5 trade and Christian-market presses talk past each other. A Christian house cannot publish a book agnostic about conversion (its retail channel punishes it); a secular imprint has lacked category confidence to publish Christianity-from-zero (it smells like evangelism). This concept is engineered for the seam: a general-imprint hardcover secular media can review as service journalism AND Christian retail can stock as the safe book for a visitor. On the two listed risks: (1) Author-dependence — mitigated by making the STRUCTURE load-bearing rather than the voice: field-guide chapters, decoder glossary, church-visit protocol, and reported convert interviews mean the book works with a very good writer, not only a once-in-a-generation one; and the required author (adult-convert or openly-still-deciding culture writer under 35) is a real, growing bench — the millennial-convert-essay wave of 2024-26 — not a unicorn hunt. The 'translator' framing widens the pool further: the author must be credible as a guide, not as an evangelist. (2) Event-driven spike — the demand line predates and exceeds any single news event: 2x growth since 2019, transatlantic (UK doubling 2019-2024), users 38%→41%, category growth in a flat market; and the product is deliberately evergreen backlist (fluency doesn't expire; the never-churched cohort only grows every year as secular-raised kids reach adulthood). Against fast-followers: the title claims the exact documented purchase moment ('so you bought a Bible' is verifiably unclaimed while blog posts squat the phrase), and a journalist rushing the trend produces another book ABOUT the revival (Ash, Brierley already exist) — the reader-facing manual is a different, harder-to-preempt genre that requires the author search this proposal already scopes.
concept.author_profile: Required: a millennial/Gen Z writer (roughly 26-38) with mainstream literary credentials, native internet fluency, and lived credibility as a translator — an adult convert from a fully secular upbringing, or an openly still-deciding seeker; NOT a pastor, apologist, or cradle Christian, whose voice re-creates the church-facing books that already exist. A light academic advisory bench (one biblical scholar, one church historian) inoculates against error without preachiness. Realistic paths: (1) the 2024-26 wave of viral millennial-convert essayists at general-interest outlets — this is now a recognizable byline type an agent can shortlist within weeks; (2) religion-vertical Substack/podcast writers under 40 with owned audiences in the tens of thousands; (3) a young religion-beat journalist at a major daily who converted on the beat; (4) fallback pairing — a literary culture writer with the scholar-advisor structure formalized as co-credit. The platform requirement is moderate, not celebrity-level, because the title and category demand do discovery work; but the author search is the critical path and should start before the proposal is finished.
marketing_hooks: Annual data-drop news peg: every Circana/publisher Bible-sales report regenerates the 'why is Gen Z buying Bibles?' cycle (already RNS, Christianity Today, transatlantic coverage) — the author becomes the booked expert with the answer, refreshing publicity every year.

Retail companion-product play: physically merchandise it as 'the second book' — endcaps and bundles beside Bible displays at B&N and Christian retail, targeting the exact 19M-unit purchase moment named in the title.

Natively clip-able chapters: 'how to visit a church without combusting' and the church-vocabulary decoder are built for the BookTok/witchtok-adjacent 'what I wish I knew before I walked into a church' content format the target cohort already consumes.

Institutional bulk channel: campus ministries, Alpha-style newcomer courses, and churches that need a non-preachy book to hand visitors — the gift-to-the-curious-friend engine that drove Strobel past 5M copies, now pointed at a book both sides can safely give.

Podcast/meaning-crisis circuit: the intellectual-Christianity conversation (Brierley's audience, meaning-crisis and secular-curious shows) is actively hungry for a young voice with a reader-facing book rather than another trend diagnosis.
Simultaneous UK co-publication: UK Bible sales nearly doubled 2019-2024 and the Ash book proved UK media appetite — a transatlantic launch doubles the review surface at minimal cost.
concept.open_questions: Honest weaknesses: (1) Single-scout evidence — unlike ranks 1-2, no independent cross-validation; the industry data is strong (Circana units, named publisher testimony) but came via search extracts and needs one primary-source confirmation pass. (2) The core conversion assumption is unproven: 'buys a Bible' does not automatically mean 'buys an explainer' — some fraction of the 19M is aesthetic/identity-driven purchasing (premium bindings, journaling Bibles) that may never seek a companion text; no direct evidence yet of explainer purchases by this cohort, only stopgap web content standing in for one. (3) The event-driven component is real: part of the 2025 spike may trace to cultural shock events (the research flags post-Kirk-assassination buying), and the durable baseline is not yet separable from the surge. (4) The seam positioning is genuinely hard to execute — Christian retail may balk at a book agnostic about conversion while secular reviewers probe it for stealth evangelism; the book must survive suspicion from both flanks, which is a marketing-copy tightrope. (5) The author search is the critical path and could take 6-12 months; a mismatched author converts this into exactly the apologetics book the gap analysis warns against, and the concept has no value without solving it. (6) Political risk: any Christianity book in the US 2026 environment risks being conscripted into culture-war coverage; the fluency-not-faith framing is the shield, but it must hold under hostile readings from both directions.

concept.subtitle: The Family Protocol That Protects Your Parents, Your Money, and Your Trust in the Age of AI Impersonation
concept.one_line_pitch: The Gift of Fear for the deepfake era: the first major-publisher book that gives families a drillable defense system — safe words, callback rituals, 24-hour money holds — against the AI voice-clone and deepfake scams that took roughly $7.7 billion from older Americans last year, sold to adult children as the book they hand their parents at Thanksgiving.
concept.category: Hardcover prescriptive nonfiction — Self-Help / Safety & Security / Personal Finance (Fraud), with Family & Relationships crossover; includes tear-out wallet card and one-page family drill; strong audio and large-print potential given the senior co-audience.
concept.target_reader: Primary buyer: Gen-X and millennial adult children (skewing women 40-60, the strongest nonfiction-buying demographic) purchasing for and with their parents — a classic 'buy it for Mom' duty/gift purchase. Secondary buyer: the 62M Americans 65+ themselves, among the most reliable print-book demographics; AARP's fraud-watch audience alone is tens of millions. Tertiary/institutional: banks, credit unions, financial advisors, estate attorneys, and senior centers buying in bulk for clients. The dual-generation structure means one scare (a news segment, a near-miss call) can convert two buyers per family.
concept.core_promise: In one weekend, your family goes from defenseless against synthetic voices and faces to running a simple, rehearsed verification system: a safe word everyone knows, a hang-up-and-call-back rule nobody breaks, a 24-hour hold on any money moved under emotion, and hardened accounts that make the worst-case loss survivable. The reader stops relying on recognition ('that's my grandson's voice') — which AI has permanently broken — and starts relying on verification, which no scammer can clone. The deeper payoff: protecting a parent without condescending to them, and preserving family trust in a world where 'seeing is believing' is over.
concept.why_now: The research file documents a 2025-26 detonation: AI-enabled scams up 1,210% in 2025 (Vectra); deepfake vishing up 1,600%+ in a single quarter; cybercrime losses among Americans 60+ hitting ~$7.7B in 2025, up 37% year over year; FBI IC3 logging 22,000+ AI-fraud complaints with $893M in adjusted losses; and voice cloning now requiring just 3 seconds of audio. Mid-2026 coverage is relentless — CNN (May 2026), Consumer Reports, AARP, CBS, Scientific American, and local TV are all running protect-yourself explainers — and the NCOA published a web guide explicitly because no book exists. The canonical consumer scam-defense book (Abagnale's Scam Me If You Can, Portfolio, 2019 — verified) predates consumer generative AI entirely; the only AI-era entrants are ISBN-979 self-pub. Every future IC3 annual report, FTC data release, and celebrity deepfake scandal regenerates the news hook through 2027 and beyond.

toc.summary: Opens with a reconstructed voice-clone 'kidnapping' call, then shows how a single public TikTok clip becomes a weapon: the mechanics of voice cloning and deepfakes explained for readers with zero technical background. Thesis: recognition is dead; verification must replace it.

How the grandparent scam became an industrialized, AI-scaled business — the scale of losses among Americans 60+ (~$7.7B in 2025, +37% YoY; 22,000+ FBI IC3 AI-fraud complaints), with every figure traced to primary institutional sources rather than the inconsistent numbers circulating in news coverage.

toc.chapter: 3. Why Smart People Send the Money
toc.summary: The psychology of urgency, authority, and love that scam scripts exploit — these attacks hijack the best parts of people, not the dumbest. Destigmatizes victimhood so families can talk about it, and dismantles the 'I'd never fall for it' overconfidence that makes the sharpest relative the softest target.

Why every legacy defense — caller ID, a familiar voice, a live video call — is now spoofable, and why protection must move from detecting fakes (a losing arms race) to protocols that work even when the fake is perfect.

The core protocol: how to choose a family safe word, who gets it, how to ask for it without tipping off an impostor, and how to drill it until asking feels automatic instead of awkward. The chapter that names and owns the practice TV segments have been giving away in 90-second fragments.

The second pillar: never act on an inbound channel. Callback rules, second-channel verification, and scripts for the moment of panic — including what to do when the 'police officer' or 'bank' says there's no time.

The money hold: no irreversible transfer — wire, gift card, crypto, cash courier — under emotion, ever. How to pre-commit as a family, why the gift-card/wire/crypto triad is a universal red flag, and how a built-in delay defeats nearly every urgency-based script regardless of the technology behind it.

Account architecture that caps the blast radius: credit freezes, transaction alerts, trusted-contact designations, view-only access for adult children, two-person rules for large transfers, and where powers of attorney fit — the difference between losing $500 and losing a life's savings.

The Thanksgiving tabletop exercise: a one-hour agenda for the family meeting, rehearsal scenarios, the wallet card, and how to make the drill an annual ritual like a fire drill — because a protocol nobody has practiced is a protocol nobody will use at 2 a.m.

The role-reversal minefield: scripts for raising fraud with a proud parent without condescension, navigating a parent who refuses help, and building the protocol WITH seniors rather than imposing it on them — autonomy-preserving language tested against the reasons these conversations usually fail.

When money is already gone: the recovery sequence (bank, IC3, FTC, local police), the honest odds of clawback and how to maximize them, gray-market 'recovery service' re-scams that prey on victims twice, and the shame spiral — how families rebuild trust after a loss.

toc.chapter: 12. The Institutions That Should Be Protecting Us
toc.summary: A reported chapter on why banks, carriers, and platforms let this happen — liability gaps, the regulatory lag, what to demand from your bank now — giving the book its argumentative spine and its policy-conversation seat.

Live video deepfakes, agentic AI scams that negotiate in real time, romance and grief bots — and why the book's protocols, which assume the fake will be perfect, don't expire when the technology improves. Tactics date; verification rituals don't.

The movement close: verification as the new household literacy, the four family norms (safe word, callback, money hold, annual drill) as this book's equivalent of Haidt's phone-free-school norms, and the case that rebuilding trust deliberately is how families win the impersonation age.

comps.title: Scam Me If You Can
comps.author: Frank Abagnale
comps.relevance: The named stale incumbent from the research: proves a mass consumer market for scam defense from a big-five imprint, and predates consumer generative AI entirely — it cannot answer the question every 2026 reader is asking. We are its direct generational replacement.

comps.title: The Anxious Generation
comps.author: Jonathan Haidt
comps.relevance: The structural playbook, per the research's own framing: alarming quantified data + a small set of named, adoptable norms + an institutional channel. Haidt owns 'phone-free schools'; this book owns 'the family safe word.' Same anxious-family buyer, adjacent shelf.

comps.title: The Gift of Fear
comps.author: Gavin de Becker
comps.relevance: The category-longevity proof: a protection book built on durable principles rather than perishable tactics has sold for nearly three decades. Directly answers the 18-month-dating risk — our protocols-not-products architecture is modeled on it.

comps.title: The Confidence Game
comps.author: Maria Konnikova
comps.relevance: Proves trade readers buy serious psychology-of-deception books; supplies the model for chapters 3-4. We add what Konnikova deliberately omits: a prescriptive system.

comps.title: The 36-Hour Day
comps.author: Nancy Mace & Peter Rabins
comps.relevance: The template for the dual-buyer family manual — adult children buying on behalf of aging parents — and for decades-long backlist life in an aging-parent category. Cited in the research file itself as the own-the-category-for-a-decade model.

concept.differentiation: Every risk the scouts flagged is answered structurally. (1) 'Tactics date in 18 months': the book's spine is four evergreen protocols (safe word, callback, money hold, annual drill) that assume the fake will be PERFECT — they get stronger, not weaker, as deepfakes improve, because they never depend on detecting the fake. Scam-of-the-month specifics are quarantined in a dated appendix and a companion wallet-card/site designed for revision, exactly how The Gift of Fear has stayed sellable since 1997. (2) 'Statistics are inconsistent across outlets': we make that the book's authority moat, not its weakness — every figure is anchored to primary institutional sources (FBI IC3 annual reports, FTC Sentinel) with ranges where sources genuinely diverge, positioning the book as the rigorous corrective to sloppy TV numbers; the scout's own honesty note becomes chapter 2's methodology. (3) 'AARP could publish the definitive version': AARP's imprint produces member-service titles, not conversation-owning trade bestsellers — Common Sense Media's existence didn't stop Haidt. Our strategy converts AARP from rival to channel: recruit an AARP Fraud Watch-affiliated expert as co-author or foreword, and note that our center of gravity — the adult child as protocol leader, the two-generation family system — sits outside AARP's senior-facing lane. Against the broader shelf: Abagnale is pre-AI; the only AI-era titles are verified ISBN-979 self-pub; NCOA published a web guide explicitly because no book exists; and the safe-word meme is currently scattered across CBS/CNN segments, a Scientific American explainer, and a National Cybersecurity Alliance web campaign (all verified) — massive validated demand for a practice with no book attached. First credible mover plants the flag on a named protocol, and a named protocol, once planted, is the hardest position for a fast-follower to attack.
concept.author_profile: Ideal: a two-hander pairing a consumer-fraud journalist (CNN/Consumer Reports/ProPublica fraud beat — the people writing the mid-2026 explainer wave) with an institutional-credibility expert (AARP Fraud Watch Network leadership, an alum of AARP's 'The Perfect Scam' podcast, or a former FBI IC3 / Secret Service financial-crimes agent). The journalist supplies narrative and reporting; the credential supplies media bookability and the institutional bulk channel. Realistic paths: the AARP Fraud Watch bench is deep, publicly visible, and largely un-agented for trade; the CNN and Consumer Reports bylines on the cited 2026 coverage are directly approachable; a bank fraud-prevention executive with media experience is the fallback. A social-engineering expert with an existing scam-content audience (podcast/YouTube scam-baiting space) is the platform-first alternative if the panel weights self-marketing. What to avoid: a pure cybersecurity technologist — this is a family-psychology book wearing a fraud book's jacket, and the voice must talk to daughters, not CISOs.
marketing_hooks: Every local-TV and cable scam segment is a free ad with no book to point to — the author becomes the standing expert guest for a beat that CNN, CBS, Consumer Reports, and AARP are already covering weekly in mid-2026.

'Family safe word' is an already-searched, already-viral phrase (CBS, CNN, Scientific American, and a National Cybersecurity Alliance campaign all push it — verified) with zero books attached: a pre-built SEO and title flag-plant.

The Thanksgiving positioning: 'the book you bring home to your parents' — a duty/gift purchase with a built-in annual season, plus a photographable tear-out wallet card and one-hour Family Drill agenda engineered for social sharing ('we did our family fire drill').

Institutional bulk channel: banks, credit unions, financial advisors, and estate attorneys already spend on fraud-prevention client education — a giftable hardcover beats their brochures; senior centers and libraries mirror the AARP/NCOA distribution the web guides already travel through.

Recurring hard news pegs: the FBI IC3 annual report every spring, FTC Sentinel releases, each new celebrity voice-clone or deepfake-fraud scandal, and October's Cybersecurity Awareness Month (whose official campaign is literally named 'Safe Word') regenerate coverage through 2027+.
The stat arsenal is made for jacket copy and TV chyrons: a 3-second clip can clone your voice; AI scams up 1,210% in one year; ~$7.7B taken from Americans 60+ in 2025 alone.
concept.open_questions: Honest weaknesses: (1) Fear-driven protection books convert unevenly — the buyer must believe a $28 book beats the free NCOA/AARP web guides; our answer (a rehearsed family SYSTEM vs. a tip list) is plausible but unproven at the register.
