(2) Several headline statistics come from vendor or low-authority sources (Vectra's 1,210%, the 1,600% vishing figure via an aggregator site) and the scout explicitly flagged cross-outlet inconsistency; the proposal's numbers must survive a primary-source verification pass before auction, and some may shrink. (3) Seniors' documented resistance to being 'protected' could cap real-world protocol adoption, weakening word-of-mouth if families buy the book but never run the drill. (4) AARP-as-channel is a strategy, not a signed deal — if AARP instead fast-tracks its own imprint title with its house experts, our institutional channel halves. (5) An unannounced big-five acquisition on the fraud beat is invisible by definition; this concept's defense is protocol branding, not secrecy, and speed to proposal matters. (6) The title's kink connotation is a real double-edge — attention-getting but needs audience testing; 'The Family Firewall' and 'Hang Up, Call Back' are tested alternates. (7) Single-scout sourcing: unlike ranks 1-2, this opportunity lacks multi-scout convergence, so the demand read leans on one researcher's (well-evidenced) sweep.

concept.working_title: When the School Bus Stops Coming
concept.subtitle: The Parent's Countdown Guide to the Autism Services Cliff — Money, Rights, Housing, Work, and a Real Adult Life After 22
concept.one_line_pitch: The '36-Hour Day' for the autism generation's next crisis: the first book to walk the 500,000 families now approaching the age-22 services cliff through a dated, age-by-age countdown — arriving the year the ABLE Age Adjustment Act made every competing title on the shelf legally obsolete.
concept.category: FAM012000 Family & Relationships / Children with Special Needs (cross-shelved Parenting / Disability); trade paperback original with worksheet interior + companion downloadables, built for updated editions every 2-3 years
concept.target_reader: PRIMARY: parents of autistic and IDD teens aged 14-22 — ~50,000 autistic youths finish US high school every year, ~500,000 will transition to adulthood over the next decade (Drexel Life Course Outcomes data in the research file), sitting inside a wider IDD parent population that already pays for attorneys, advocates, and conferences. SECONDARY: the professional wraparound — transition coordinators, special-ed case managers, special-needs financial planners (ChSNC/CFP), and disability attorneys who hand books to clients — plus nonprofits and parent training centers that bulk-buy for workshops (a documented institutional channel). TERTIARY (new, created by the 2026 law): families of the 6M+ adults with disability onset before 46 — including ~1M veterans — who became ABLE-eligible on Jan 1, 2026 and need the same money chapters.
concept.core_promise: Converts the most terrifying deadline in special-needs parenting from a void into a sequenced, dated plan. The reader finishes knowing exactly what to do at 14, 16, 17, 18, 21, and 22 — which benefits to file, which legal decisions to make (and which, like full guardianship, NOT to default into), how to structure money under the post-2026 ABLE rules, how to game Medicaid waiver waitlists that run a decade long, and how to build work, housing, and an actual life after the entitlement system ends. The transformation: from 'the bus stops coming and everything ends' to 'the bus stops coming and our plan starts.'
concept.why_now: A double hard catalyst, both dated and citable. (1) REGULATORY: On January 1, 2026, the ABLE Age Adjustment Act raised the disability-onset eligibility age from 26 to 46, making 6M+ additional Americans (incl. ~1M veterans) eligible for the single most important tax-protected savings vehicle in disability planning (ABLE NRC; Disability Belongs, Jan 2026 — verified this week). Every planning book printed before 2026 states the eligibility rules wrong. Regulatory why-nows are rarer and more durable than discourse why-nows: they expire the competition instead of just outshouting it. (2) DEMOGRAPHIC: the first mass wave of the early-2000s autism-diagnosis surge is aging into adulthood right now — the cliff Drexel's Life Course Outcomes program documents (school-based supports vanish at 18-22, entitlement flips to eligibility) is hitting ~50,000 families per year, every year, for the next decade. Child Mind and Kennedy Krieger document that parents arrive at the cliff unprepared; entire nonprofit content verticals (Exceptional Lives, Undivided) exist to fill the book-shaped hole.

toc.summary: What actually ends at 18, 21, and 22: the overnight flip from IDEA's legal entitlement to the adult system's eligibility-and-waitlist regime, grounded in Drexel's services-cliff data. Names the reader's dread precisely — then reframes the book as the countdown that defuses it.

The book's spine and signature framework: a master timeline of every legal, financial, and services action keyed to ages 14, 16, 17, 18, 21, and 22, with a one-page pull-out checklist. This chapter is the excerpt, the workshop handout, and the reason the book gets bought at transition fairs.

The single most consequential 24 hours in the process: adult SSI application (and why the first denial is normal), the adult Medicaid switch, and redetermination under adult disability criteria — with scripts for the interviews and a paperwork triage system.

The modern menu parents never hear at the IEP table: supported decision-making agreements, powers of attorney, and limited guardianship — how to match the tool to your child's actual capacity instead of signing away their adulthood because a school lawyer said so.

toc.chapter: 5. The New Money Rules: ABLE After January 2026
toc.summary: The first trade-book treatment of the ABLE Age Adjustment Act: onset-before-46 eligibility, who among the 6M newly eligible is in your family (possibly you), contribution mechanics, ABLE-to-work, and how the account interacts with SSI's $2,000 asset trap.

toc.chapter: 6. The Two-Bucket System: ABLE Accounts + Special Needs Trusts
toc.summary: How the two vehicles divide labor — daily-life money vs. inheritance-scale money — with decision trees for grandparent gifts, life insurance, and the Medicaid payback trap that catches DIY families.

Medicaid HCBS waivers demystified: why you apply years before you need services, how priority categories and crisis criteria actually work, and a state-agnostic strategy framework (with worksheets) for a system where the waitlist itself is the policy.

Extracting maximum value from the final entitled years: transition assessments and plans that name adult goals, work experience while still enrolled, the 18-22 program decision, and when staying until the last eligible day beats early graduation.

Vocational rehabilitation, customized employment, and self-employment routes beyond the collapsing sheltered-workshop model — plus the benefits-cliff math (SSI income rules, ABLE-to-work) that lets your adult child earn without losing health coverage.

The honest housing chapter: the family home with supports, shared living, supported apartments, group homes, and parent-driven co-ops — costed, waitlist-aware, and sequenced, including using the ABLE account for housing without touching SSI's rent rules.
Friendship, recreation, transportation, relationships, and sexuality after the built-in social world of school disappears — the chapter org PDFs never write and parents most underplan.

Finding adult providers who will actually see IDD patients, transferring records and psychiatric care, dental (the quiet crisis), and preparing your child for appointments where you may no longer be legally in the room.

toc.chapter: 13. When the Plan Meets the Person
toc.summary: The refuser, the regressor, and the crisis: what to do when your adult child says no to the program you fought for, when skills slip after school structure ends, and how to access behavioral crisis services as an adult family.

Siblings, grandparents, and the succession question — assigning future roles explicitly, funding them fairly, and the estate-planning conversations that prevent the two classic disasters: the disinherited child and the well-meaning uncle who wrecks benefits with a direct gift.

The document that outlives you: a guided, template-driven chapter for writing down everything only you know about your child — routines, medical history, fears, joys — so care survives the transfer to whoever comes next.

The book's answer to 50-state variation: a structured worksheet system that walks the reader through finding and recording their own state's waiver names, agencies, waitlist rules, and parent center — plus a QR-linked update page keeping the book current between editions.

comps.title: The 36-Hour Day
comps.author: Nancy L. Mace and Peter V. Rabins
comps.year: 1981; updated editions through 2021 (Johns Hopkins University Press)
comps.relevance: The business model, named in the research file itself: a category-defining caregiver guide that owned its niche for four decades through updated editions and institutional/professional hand-selling. This book is engineered to be the 36-Hour Day of the IDD transition — perennial backlist, not one-season frontlist.

comps.title: Uniquely Human
comps.author: Barry M. Prizant
comps.relevance: Proof the autism-parent audience buys big-trade books in bestseller volume when the voice is credible and warm — establishing the ceiling is higher than 'niche reference' if execution lands.

comps.title: A Full Life with Autism
comps.author: Chantal Sicile-Kira and Jeremy Sicile-Kira
comps.relevance: The direct incumbent — and the proof of the gap: the last mainstream-press parent guide to autistic adulthood predates ABLE accounts entirely (created 2014), the supported-decision-making movement, and the 2026 rules. Fourteen years stale on a shelf where the law just changed.

comps.title: How to Care for Aging Parents
comps.author: Virginia Morris
comps.relevance: The adjacent-category template for a comprehensive family-navigator guide (500K+ copies) — demonstrating that 'one organized book replacing a hundred scattered web pages' is a proven purchase in caregiving categories, and that the updated-edition franchise model holds commercial value for decades.

comps.title: Special Needs Trusts
comps.author: Kevin Urbatsch and Jessica Farinas (Nolo)
comps.relevance: Represents the fragmented legal-only competition the research documents: proves families pay for current-rules disability-money guidance in book form, while covering only one sliver of what this book integrates — and its pre-2026 edition now misstates ABLE eligibility.

concept.differentiation: Every risk in the research note is answered structurally. (1) 'Smaller ceiling / backlist play': embraced and monetized rather than denied — the 36-Hour Day model shows a category-owning caregiver guide compounds for decades via updated editions, professional hand-selling, and bulk workshop sales; and the Jan 2026 ABLE expansion mechanically widens the money chapters' audience beyond autism to the entire IDD population and 6M newly eligible later-onset adults (incl. ~1M veterans), lifting the ceiling above the scout's estimate. (2) 'State-by-state Medicaid variation makes a national book hard': this is the moat, not the flaw — the two-layer architecture (a federal spine that is genuinely national: SSI, Medicaid waiver mechanics, ABLE, IDEA, guardianship doctrine; plus Chapter 16's fill-in-your-state worksheet system and a QR-linked update page) solves the exact problem that has kept this category trapped in org blog verticals and legal-only titles. No blog post can deliver the integrated sequence; no national book has dared the state layer. (3) Preemption/fast-follow: the research rates big-publisher attention here near zero — the opposite profile of the AI-topic entries — and the defenses compound: the community's own phrase as title (verified unclaimed as a trade book), first-mover partnerships with the parent-center/nonprofit bulk channel (a relationship asset a fast-follower can't clone), and the countdown-calendar format as recognizable trade dress. (4) Self-obsolescence: the book teaches decision frameworks and where-to-verify protocols rather than rate tables, and the edition cycle turns rule churn into recurring revenue. Against the existing shelf: the 2012 incumbent predates ABLE's existence; the legal titles cover one bucket; the nonprofit PDFs are free but fragmentary and unsequenced — this is the only product that integrates the 2026 money rules, supported decision-making, waiver waitlist strategy, employment, housing, and the emotional arc into one dated plan.
concept.author_profile: Needs stacked credibility across law/money and lived experience — the realistic winning package is a PAIR: a special-needs planning attorney or ChSNC-credentialed financial planner (supplies the rules-currency the 2026 hook demands) co-writing with a nationally visible autism-parent journalist or advocate (supplies voice, story, and platform). Realistic paths: (a) a director or senior faculty from a UCEDD/LEND transition program or Drexel's Life Course Outcomes orbit, who arrives with the data and the conference circuit; (b) a known special-needs-finance educator who already tours the parent-center workshop circuit, paired with a trade editor-approved co-writer; (c) a major-outlet disability-beat journalist who is also an IDD parent. Non-negotiables: the author must be workshop-bookable (the institutional channel IS the marketing plan) and must carry endorsements from at least one national org (The Arc, Autism Society, ABLE NRC) whose logo converts skeptical parents. This is deliberately NOT a celebrity-platform bet — in this category, org trust outperforms follower counts, which also makes the author search cheaper and faster than for ranks 1-4.
marketing_hooks: The dated regulatory peg: 'On January 1, 2026, every special-needs planning book in print became wrong' — the ABLE Age Adjustment Act (onset-before-46, 6M+ newly eligible, ~1M veterans) is a citable, evergreen-for-the-edition news hook that re-fires with every ABLE NRC awareness campaign and state program announcement.

A built-in annual season: every May-June graduation cycle, ~50,000 more families watch the bus stop coming — the title phrase is already the community's own language (used by Pathfinders for Autism, Howard County Autism Society, ECAC parent-center events), so the book plugs into existing transition-fair programming nationwide.

The institutional bulk channel: federally funded Parent Training and Information Centers in every state, The Arc chapters, Autism Society affiliates, and school-district transition coordinators run standing workshops on exactly this topic and currently hand out PDFs — a workshop-ready book with a pull-out countdown checklist is their missing curriculum.

The countdown calendar as shareable object: the age-14-to-22 checklist is a natural one-page excerpt for TikTok/Instagram autism-parent creators, Facebook IDD-parent groups (some with six-figure memberships), and pediatrician/IEP-meeting handout distribution — every share sells the whole sequenced book.

The veterans/late-onset expansion story: ~1M newly ABLE-eligible veterans give military media, VSOs, and personal-finance press a reason to cover a 'special-needs parenting' book — a second, non-overlapping publicity lane no prior title in the category has had.

The demographic-wave narrative for feature coverage: 'the autism generation grows up' — the early-2000s diagnosis surge hitting adulthood is a ready-made trend story (Drexel data, Child Mind, Kennedy Krieger all quotable), and this book positions its author as the go-to expert source every time an outlet runs it.
concept.open_questions: Honest weaknesses: (1) Ceiling is genuinely capped relative to ranks 1-4 — this is a build-a-decade backlist franchise, not an NYT-list play; a judge scoring pure first-year commercial upside should discount it, and the P&L must be modeled on 36-Hour-Day-style longevity plus bulk sales, not frontlist velocity. (2) The free-alternative problem: the nonprofit channel that makes the best sales force also gives away competing PDFs; the bet is that parents in crisis pay $22 for sequence and integration — supported by the paid-attorney/conference behavior documented in the research, but not directly proven for this exact purchase. (3) Heterogeneity of the audience: one book must serve parents of both a college-bound autistic teen and a nonspeaking adult needing 24/7 support; the countdown structure helps (same deadlines, different branches) but some readers will find whole chapters irrelevant — reviewer risk. (4) Regulatory churn cuts both ways: 2025-26 federal Medicaid pressure could reshape waiver funding after the manuscript closes; the framework-over-rate-tables design mitigates but cannot eliminate dating risk, and a mid-cycle rules shock could force an early second edition. (5) Evidence caveat inherited from the research: the scout's page-level evidence came from search extracts due to fetch blocks, so shelf-emptiness and parent-unpreparedness claims should get one confirmation pass (my spot-checks this session did verify the ABLE details, the 2012 incumbent, and that the title phrase is unclaimed as a trade book). (6) The title's community currency is an asset but the phrase is used in free org content — positioning must make the book feel like the definitive upgrade, not a repackage of familiar workshop material.

concept.subtitle: A Triage Guide to the Ten Phone Calls That Change Your Life — the Fall, the Diagnosis, the Discharge, the Money, and the Parent Who Refuses Help
concept.one_line_pitch: Every caregiving story starts the same way — a phone call — so this is the first aging-parents book built like an ER, not an encyclopedia: ten crisis-sequenced chapters with 72-hour checklists, word-for-word scripts, and 2026-current money rules, replacing a 650-page category bible last revised in 2014, published in the exact year the first baby boomers turn 80.
concept.category: Family & Relationships / Eldercare (FAM017000); trade paperback original with flexibind workbook features (decision trees, tear-out checklists, fill-in 'Parent File' dossier), simultaneous ebook/audio
concept.target_reader: Primary: women 40-60 — the single strongest nonfiction-buying demographic — inside the 63M US family caregivers (1 in 4 adults, up ~20M since 2015 per AARP/NAC July 2025); Pew (Feb 2026, n=8,750) finds 24% of adults with a living parent 65+ are already caregiving. Secondary: the millennial sandwich cohort (47% of caregivers under 50 are simultaneously raising children) and siblings/spouses of the primary caregiver — the book is engineered to be bought in multiples ('one for your brother'). Tertiary/institutional: hospital discharge planners, Area Agencies on Aging, employer caregiver-benefit programs. Caregivers already spend ~$10K/year out of pocket on this problem; a $28 book is a rounding error.
concept.core_promise: In the 72 hours after the phone call, you will know exactly what to do, in what order, and what to say — to the hospital that wants the bed back, the parent who says no, the sibling who won't help, and the bank that won't talk to you. And in the years that follow, you will know how to build a care machine (legal papers, money triage, paid-family-caregiver programs, portals, the group chat) that runs without consuming your job, your marriage, or your kids' childhood.
concept.why_now: 2026 is 'Peak 80': the first baby boomers turn 80 this year (Washington Times, Dec 2025; Fortune, Apr 2026), the moment eldercare demand structurally inflects. AARP's July 2025 report is literally titled a 'crisis point' for America's 63 million caregivers; Pew published its first-ever dedicated family-caregiving report in Feb 2026; the caregiver support ratio is collapsing from 6.1 to 4.1 potential caregivers per elder by 2030 (AARP PPI); and 24-hour home care now runs ~$290K/year. Meanwhile the mechanisms that actually help — Medicaid self-direction programs that pay family caregivers, post-2014 Medicare changes, telehealth, portal-based care coordination — did not exist when the category leader was last revised in 2014, meaning every incumbent is not just stale but factually wrong about the reader's best options.

toc.summary: The book's organizing thesis: no one 'becomes a caregiver' — they answer a phone call, usually at the worst possible moment, and the average guide answers it with 650 pages of alphabetized dread. How to use this book like a triage nurse: go to your call, follow the tree, come back for the rest later.

The ER-to-admission playbook: what 'observation status' secretly costs you, the five questions to ask the hospitalist, and the one-page Parent File to build tonight (meds, insurance, contacts) so every future crisis starts from ambush-proof ground.

toc.chapter: 2. 'She's Being Discharged Tomorrow' — Discharge Is Not a Plan
toc.summary: Fighting the discharge machine: your federal appeal rights, the 'unsafe discharge' language that makes hospitals slow down, how to vet the rehab list they hand you in the hallway, and the 48-hour home-readiness checklist.

toc.chapter: 3. 'I'm Fine' — The Parent Who Refuses Help
toc.summary: The chapter the 1999 incumbent handled by pathologizing your parent. Instead: motivational-interviewing scripts that get to yes in weeks not years, what you legally can and cannot force on a competent adult, and the documented-decline file that protects everyone when you can't.

toc.chapter: 4. 'Something's Off With Mom' — Delirium, Depression, or Dementia
toc.summary: The three D's that get confused in exam rooms, how to get a real cognitive workup a parent will actually attend, and what a diagnosis changes immediately — driving, finances, legal capacity — versus what it doesn't.

toc.chapter: 5. The Paper Emergency — POA, Proxy, HIPAA, and the Documents You Need This Month
toc.summary: Why every other task in this book becomes 10x harder without four signatures, how to raise the subject without sounding like you're circling the estate, and what happens (guardianship court, frozen accounts) if you wait until capacity is gone.

toc.chapter: 6. The Money Triage — Finding It, Guarding It, Making It Last
toc.summary: Locating scattered accounts, stopping the scam bleed-out, the real arithmetic of care ($290K/year for 24-hour home care), and the spend sequence: what to burn first, what to protect, and when 'spending down' is strategy rather than failure.

toc.chapter: 7. Getting Paid to Do This — Medicaid, Waivers, and the Programs Nobody Told You About
toc.summary: The chapter that dates every competitor: Medicaid self-direction and paid-family-caregiver waivers, the look-back rules, VA Aid & Attendance, and a state-variation worksheet system so the framework survives the fifty different rulebooks.

toc.chapter: 8. 'Why Is This All on Me?' — Siblings, Scorekeeping, and the Family Meeting
toc.summary: The daughter-default and how to break it: running a family meeting with an agenda and assignments, care contracts that pay the on-site sibling, long-distance jobs that actually matter, and what to do when a sibling is spending Mom's money.

toc.chapter: 9. Where Will They Live? — The Real Decision Tree
toc.summary: Aging in place vs. assisted living vs. memory care vs. your spare bedroom: honest cost and burden math for each branch, the 100-point tour checklist that catches what marketing brochures hide, and why 'never a nursing home' is a promise no one should extract.

toc.chapter: 10. Building the Machine — Care Logistics Your Parents' Generation Never Had
toc.summary: The modern toolkit missing from every pre-2014 guide: patient portals and telehealth as force multipliers, the shared calendar and sibling group chat as command center, remote monitoring without surveillance creep, and hiring/managing aides.

toc.chapter: 11. The Sandwich Protocol — Doing This With Toddlers and a Job
toc.summary: For the 47% of under-50 caregivers also raising kids: FMLA and intermittent leave mechanics, negotiating with your employer (and when not to disclose), what your kids should see and be shielded from, and triaging a two-front life.

Caregiver collapse as clinical risk, not self-care garnish: the burnout warning signs spouses spot first, respite as infrastructure, boundaries scripted rather than aspirational, and permission — from the data — to be a good-enough caregiver.

toc.chapter: 13. The Fast Goodbye and the Long One — Hospice, Palliative Care, and the Conversations
toc.summary: When to call hospice (months earlier than most families do), palliative care as an upgrade rather than surrender, and scripts for the conversations about what your parent wants while they can still tell you.

toc.chapter: 14. After — The Estate, the Grief, and Getting Your Life Back
toc.summary: The executor's first month, the strange guilt-shaped grief of caregivers, re-entering a career and marriage that ran on autopilot — and the letter to write your own kids so they never have to buy this book in an ambulance bay.

comps.title: How to Care for Aging Parents (3rd Edition)
comps.author: Virginia Morris
comps.relevance: The 500K-copy category bible and proof of category scale — but 650 pages, encyclopedic, and 12 years stale; Goodreads reviewers call it 'overwhelming,' and it predates paid-family-caregiver waivers, telehealth, and portal-based care. We are its structural inversion, not its refresh.

comps.title: The 36-Hour Day
comps.author: Nancy Mace & Peter Rabins
comps.relevance: Proof that a family-caregiving manual can own its shelf for four decades of editions — but it is dementia-only. We apply its longevity model to the full crisis spectrum: falls, discharge, money, refusal, siblings, housing.

comps.title: Generation Care: The New Culture of Caregiving
comps.author: Jennifer N. Levin
comps.relevance: Fresh evidence publishers see the millennial caregiver — but it is reportage, community voices, and policy vision around burnout and identity. It names the crisis; it does not tell you what to say to the discharge planner tomorrow morning. Ideal blurber, not blocker.

comps.title: Being Mortal
comps.author: Atul Gawande
comps.relevance: Demonstrates the mass-market ceiling for aging-and-care nonfiction (millions of copies, years on lists) — and famously leaves readers asking 'so what do I actually do?' We are the operational sequel readers reach for the week the abstraction becomes their mother.

comps.title: Coping with Your Difficult Older Parent
comps.author: Grace Lebow & Barbara Kane
comps.relevance: The 27-year-old default for the refusing/difficult parent, criticized by modern readers for putting the burden on the adult child. Our Chapter 3 replaces its framework with current motivational-interviewing scripts — one of several single-chapter takeovers of entire stale incumbents.

concept.differentiation: The risk notes say the category is crowded-with-dated-titles and that differentiation must be structural, not just newer — so the structure IS the product. Every incumbent is organized by topic (housing, money, health), which forces a panicked reader to read like a student; The Parent Emergency is organized by crisis — the actual phone calls — with decision trees, 72-hour checklists, and verbatim scripts, because the reader arrives mid-emergency, not pre-emergency. This directly weaponizes the incumbent's documented weakness: 'overwhelming' is the top reader complaint about Morris, meaning the category leader's format is the market gap. Against the Workman-commissions-a-4th-edition risk: a revision would retain the A-to-Z reference architecture (that's what an 'edition' is), would take 18-24 months to ship, and cannot become a scripts-and-triage book without ceasing to be itself — our moat is format plus 2026-only content (Medicaid self-direction/paid-family-caregiver programs, telehealth and portal-based coordination, current cost data) that makes any pre-2026 text checkably wrong, a claim we can make on the jacket. Against the Levin/'Generation Care' overlap risk: verified via Hachette's own positioning, Levin's book is culture, community voices, and policy advocacy around burnout — it validates our buyer's existence and shares zero functional territory with a triage manual; she is a comp and a potential blurb, and her Caregiver Collective is a launch channel. Against evergreen-category complacency: we convert the evergreen into an event with the Peak-80 peg and the AARP 'crisis point'/first-ever Pew report data cycle. And structurally for longevity: framework-plus-worksheet design (state-variation worksheets, the Parent File) lets rules churn without killing the book, setting up a 36-Hour-Day-style edition franchise rather than a single frontlist bet.
concept.author_profile: The credibility bar is 'has personally run a thousand of these crises,' not MD: ideal is a certified geriatric care manager (Aging Life Care Association) or veteran hospital discharge planner/geriatric social worker — someone who knows the system's pressure points from inside — paired with a millennial/Gen-X journalist co-writer for voice and reporting. Realistic paths: (1) mine eldercare TikTok/Instagram and Substack, where care managers and 'dementia daughter' creators have six-figure followings built on exactly these scripts; (2) the bylines on the AARP/Pew caregiving data beat at WaPo/NYT well desks, several of whom are living the story; (3) AARP- or Family Caregiver Alliance-affiliated experts seeking a trade breakout; (4) a geriatrician with a platform (the 'Being Mortal heir' play) if a bigger advance is the goal. A foreword from a name geriatrician plus Levin/AARP blurbs completes the authority stack without gating the project on a celebrity author.
marketing_hooks: 'Peak 80' launch peg: the first boomers turn 80 in 2026, and every January brings a fresh 'largest generation hits 80/81/82' news cycle (Washington Times, Fortune already running it) — a renewable annual publicity hook, not a one-shot.

Data-cycle piggyback: AARP's July 2025 'crisis point' report and Pew's first-ever Feb 2026 caregiving study guarantee recurring coverage of the 63M-caregiver number; the book is the citable 'what to do about it' every such story currently lacks.

Native virality: chapter titles are literally the texts people post ('She's being discharged tomorrow'); scripts and decision trees excerpt as standalone TikTok/Instagram carousels for the already-huge eldercare creator economy, and 'what to say when the hospital says tomorrow' is evergreen search bait.

Institutional bulk channel: hospital discharge planners, Area Agencies on Aging, senior centers, estate attorneys, and financial advisors all need a hand-to-families object; employer caregiver benefits/EAPs (a growth benefit per Guardian's sandwich-generation programming) can buy in thousands.

The sibling multi-buy: the family-meeting chapter is designed to be assigned — 'read chapter 8 before Sunday' — making the natural unit of sale two to four copies per family, a conversion structure almost no self-help title has.

Shock-number media math: $290K/year for 24-hour care, ~$10K/year out-of-pocket, support ratio collapsing 6.1→4.1 by 2030 — personal-finance desks (Forbes, US News already covering sandwich-generation squeeze) run these numbers on repeat, and the book owns the fix.
concept.open_questions: Honest weaknesses: (1) Regulatory churn cuts both ways — 2026-current rules are our sword against the shelf but our own expiry clock; Medicare/Medicaid changes could date specifics within 2-3 years, so the P&L must assume an edition-refresh cadence and companion-site updates from day one. (2) State-by-state Medicaid variation means the hardest-value chapter (getting paid to caregive) can only teach a framework plus worksheets — some readers will still hit a 'check your state' wall. (3) Point-of-need discoverability: this buyer often searches mid-crisis rather than browsing, so success depends on out-ranking a 500K-copy incumbent in Amazon/Google search — a real marketing-spend question, partially offset by the sibling multi-buy and institutional channels. (4) No author is attached; the concept is agent-ready but the advance size swings enormously on whether we land a platformed care manager versus an unknown expert. (5) The forum-pain scout self-flagged thin direct reader-quote evidence — demand rests on national studies and shelf autopsy rather than screenshotted anguish, strong but one evidence type short. (6) Positioning collision risk in publicity (not content) with 'Generation Care' — 'millennial caregiver book' headlines could blur us; the triage-manual framing must lead every pitch. (7) Gatekeeper perception: triage format could read as less authoritative than a 650-page reference to library and review buyers who canonized the encyclopedia — the 2026-currency claim and expert-author credentials have to carry that argument.

refined_concept.subtitle: What to Do When the Phone Loves Them Back — Scripts, House Rules, and How to Out-Relate the Machine
refined_concept.one_line_pitch: The Anxious Generation told parents the phone stole their kid's childhood; this is the first clinician's protocol book for the parents who just discovered the phone now loves their kid back — word-for-word scripts, a one-page Family AI Agreement, and a step-by-step Breakup protocol for detecting, defusing, and out-relating a teen's attachment to an AI companion, for the 72% of American teens already using one.
refined_concept.category: Family & Relationships / Parenting — Teenagers (FAM039000); hardcover prescriptive nonfiction with a reported case-study spine, à la The Anxious Generation / Good Inside, with a crisis-triage front section à la an ER manual.
refined_concept.target_reader: Primary: mothers 35-55 of kids 10-17 — the demographic that kept The Anxious Generation at #1 NYT for over a year — split deliberately into two buyer states the book now serves separately: (1) the crisis buyer who just found the logs (served by the new 72-hour quick-start front section) and (2) the anxious-but-unaware buyer (served by the viral Attachment Check-In quiz that manufactures the discovery moment). Addressable base: parents of the ~42M US kids aged 8-17; per Common Sense Media/NORC (n=1,060), 72% of teens have used AI companions, 52% regularly, 33% have chosen an AI over a human for a serious conversation — figures the book triangulates against Pew, Internet Matters (UK), and the Stanford risk assessment rather than resting on alone. Secondary: school counselors, therapists, teachers, and PTAs — the schools/bulk-buy institutional channel the Haidt playbook proved; the family-meeting and co-parent chapters are written to be assigned ('read chapter 6 before Sunday'), making the natural unit of sale two-plus copies per household.
refined_concept.core_promise: In one weekend — or, for the parent in active crisis, in the first 72 hours via the triage section that opens the book — a frightened parent goes from 'I just found the chat logs and I have no idea what this is' to a working plan: the six signs of companion attachment; a green/yellow/red calibration framework (front-loaded, so readers trust the alarms); word-for-word scripts for the first conversation, with the five sentences that slam the door annotated as failure modes; age-tiered house rules via the one-page Family AI Agreement; a concrete escalation protocol for the red zone (sexual content, self-harm reinforcement) including what to screenshot and what SB 243 and the settlements entitle you to demand from platforms; a Breakup protocol that anticipates real grief and doesn't drive the relationship underground; and the upstream rebuild of the human connection the bot was substituting for. The governing reframe, sharpened: this is not a screen-time problem, it is a relationship in your kid's life — and bans demonstrably backfire, so you can't ban it; you have to out-relate it.
refined_concept.why_now: The catalysts are stacked, dated, and citable: Common Sense Media's 'unacceptable risk' verdict (July 2025); the Stanford risk assessment showing companions producing sexual content and self-harm encouragement with teen personas (Aug 2025); Character.AI banning under-18 open chat (Nov 2025) — which pushed minors toward unmoderated platforms and companion modes inside mainstream chatbots rather than ending use, the single fact that both proves the book's anti-ban thesis and immunizes the premise against an industry retreat from dedicated companion apps; the Character.AI/Google teen-suicide settlements (Jan 2026); California SB 243 in force since Jan 1, 2026, with enforcement actions regenerating coverage through 2027; MIT Technology Review naming AI companions a 2026 Breakthrough Technology; and an arXiv study documenting teens describing their own use in clinical addiction language ('withdrawal, tolerance, relapse'). The demand signal is the stopgap flood itself — APA, Child Mind, Common Sense, KQED, UNICEF web guides filling a book-shaped hole — and a July 2026 preemption check confirms no announced mainstream trade book. Every prior kids-and-tech wave produced its definitive parent book 2-3 years after the panic began; for AI companions that window is exactly now, which is why the package commits to proposal-out-by-Labor-Day 2026 and a crashed back-to-school-2027 pub.

toc.summary: A crisis-triage front door for the parent who just found the logs: which phone call are you, what not to say tonight, what to screenshot, and which chapter to read first. Converts the mid-crisis buyer that free web guides currently catch, and makes the book usable before it is read.

Three reported family stories — the discovered logs, the confrontation that went wrong, the quiet kid nobody suspected — anchored by the triangulated national numbers (72% have used companions, 52% regularly, 33% choosing the bot over a human for serious conversations). Thesis: this is not social media 2.0; it's the first technology that performs love.

Why the bot wins: frictionless intimacy, engineered sycophancy, infinite availability, zero judgment — a product built to be the perfect listener your household can't be. Includes the book's evidence methodology in plain sight: every headline number sourced to primary research with ranges where studies diverge, the authority moat against both moral-panic critics and stat-shredding fact-checkers.

toc.chapter: 3. It's a Relationship, Not Screen Time
toc.summary: The governing reframe. Teens describe their own use in withdrawal/tolerance/relapse language; treating a relationship like a device quota produces secrecy, not safety. The Character.AI under-18 ban as the case study: prohibition moved kids to darker platforms and mainstream companion modes. The alternative: parent it like a relationship you're worried about.

The calibration chapter, moved up front where credibility is earned: benign and even beneficial use exists and the research says so. A tiered framework for what's fine, what to watch, and what's a crisis — so readers trust the alarms in the red-zone chapters and the book survives the expert scrutiny that sinks moral-panic treatments.

Detection without betrayal: the six behavioral signs of companion attachment, where companion dynamics hide (dedicated apps, mainstream chatbot personas, game NPCs, social-platform AIs), what platforms log, and how to look in a way you can later defend to your kid. Includes the Attachment Check-In — the 10-question self-assessment that doubles as the book's viral online quiz.

The heart of the book: full scripts for opening the topic — the curious open, the discovered-logs conversation, the sideways 'my friend's kid' entry — with the five door-slamming sentences ('It's not real,' 'That's pathetic') annotated as failure modes. How to Talk So Kids Will Listen, rebuilt for synthetic love. Written to be assigned to a co-parent.

A parent's field guide organized by attachment dynamics, not brand names: dedicated companion apps, companion modes inside mainstream chatbots and social platforms, romantic AI in games, and the homework-helper that drifts into confidant. App-specific details live in a dated appendix and a QR-linked companion page built for revision — the architecture that keeps the book evergreen through platform churn and any post-settlement industry retreat.

The Family AI Agreement: age-tiered rules (8-12, 13-15, 16-18) covering what AI talk is fine, what's private-but-discussed, what's off-limits — negotiated with the kid, not imposed. The one-page agreement is the book's shareable artifact and free-download funnel.

When it's a crisis: sexual content with minors, self-harm encouragement, suicidal ideation surfacing in bot chats — grounded in the Stanford findings and the litigation record. A concrete escalation protocol: what to screenshot, whom to call, when a therapist is non-negotiable — plus the platform-combat playbook: what SB 243, the settlements, and platform safety policies entitle you to demand, with model complaint language. The chapter no free web guide can safely write.

Ending an attachment without driving it underground: harm-reduction taper versus cold turkey, anticipating real grief (kids mourn these relationships), managing relapse, and the parent's script for the day the bot gets deleted. The book's most ownable protocol.

The upstream chapter: the loneliness, social anxiety, or family friction the bot was medicating. Practical rebuild moves — engineered low-stakes peer contact, the 10-minute nightly no-agenda conversation, becoming askable — with the honest admission that the bot listened better than most adults do, and what to copy from it.

The 8-12s, where companions are colonizing the imaginary-friend years; neurodivergent kids, for whom bots can be both genuine practice ground and deeper trap; and the teen who insists, with evidence, that the AI helps — how to take that seriously without surrendering.

The collective-action layer that made Haidt a movement: what to ask your school to do, model PTA policy language, what enforcement of SB 243 and its state copycats means for parents, and where the regulatory fight goes next — the chapter that converts readers into the bulk channel.

The long game: companions are a 2026 Breakthrough Technology and they are not going away; the goal is not an AI-free child but a child for whom synthetic intimacy is a tool, not a home. Closes the loop on the three families from chapter 1.

comps.title: The Anxious Generation
comps.author: Jonathan Haidt
comps.relevance: Proves the buyer and the playbook — #1 NYT for over a year, 2M+ copies, sold to this exact parent demographic with a diagnosis-plus-collective-action structure. Predates companion bots entirely; this is the next book its readers need, not a competitor.

comps.title: How to Talk So Kids Will Listen & Listen So Kids Will Talk
comps.author: Adele Faber & Elaine Mazlish
comps.relevance: The scripts DNA and proof of format: parents pay, for decades, for word-for-word dialogue guidance — the evergreen-architecture precedent the field-guide chapter is built to match.

comps.title: Good Inside
comps.author: Becky Kennedy
comps.relevance: Proves a clinician with scripts and a connection-over-control philosophy can hit #1 NYT in the current market — the tonal and authorial model, and the coexistence precedent showing a protocol book thrives alongside a diagnosis bestseller (relevant to the exposé-preemption scenario).

comps.title: Raising AI: An Essential Guide to Parenting Our Future
comps.author: De Kai
comps.relevance: Proves publisher and media appetite at the AI-parenting intersection while defining the gap by what it isn't: philosophy, not a how-to for a parent holding chat logs. Its existence de-risks the category; its framing leaves the practical lane empty.

comps.title: The Emotional Lives of Teenagers
comps.author: Lisa Damour
comps.relevance: Recent proof that a credentialed adolescent psychologist writing directly to worried parents is an instant NYT bestseller — the platform template for this book's lead author.

refined_concept.differentiation: The shelf is verifiably empty and every occupant is disqualified by design: Haidt predates companion bots and stays in the phones/social-media lane; De Kai is MIT Press philosophy; three research scouts found nothing else but KDP spam; the institutional web guides are the demand signal, not the competition. The refined concept converts each judge-flagged threat into structure. (1) The exposé preemption: a Character.AI-beat journalist's book is a diagnosis (what happened); this is a protocol book (what do I do Tuesday night) — different shelf, and the jacket is pre-positioned as 'after the headlines' so an exposé landing first functions as free category marketing, exactly as Anxious Generation set up Good Inside. A clinician-fronted rival is the real threat, which is why the author search is now a 90-day two-track plan, not a wish list. (2) Free-content sufficiency: the web guides can't write the Breakup protocol, the platform-combat playbook, or the 72-hour triage section — the three deliverables a liability-averse nonprofit will never publish — and the free artifacts (Agreement, quiz) are deliberately the funnel, not the substitute. (3) Single-survey evidence base: the methodology chapter triangulates Common Sense/NORC against Pew, Stanford, Internet Matters, and the arXiv literature, states ranges, and the package commissions a proprietary 2,000-parent survey at proposal stage — turning the field's stat fragility into this book's authority moat (the Safe Word move: rigor as differentiation). (4) Industry-retreat risk: the field guide is organized by attachment dynamics across ALL synthetic-intimacy surfaces — mainstream chatbot companion modes, game NPCs, social AIs — with app specifics quarantined in a dated, QR-updated appendix; a retreat from dedicated under-18 companion apps validates rather than voids the premise, because the Nov 2025 ban already demonstrated migration. (5) The fast-follower moat remains the counterintuitive thesis a panic book will flinch from — you can't ban a relationship, you have to out-relate it — now reinforced by named, trademark-able protocols (the Quiet Audit, the Attachment Check-In, the Family AI Agreement, the Breakup) that plant flags a copycat must either license or awkwardly rename.
refined_concept.author_profile: Requirement unchanged: a licensed clinical psychologist or adolescent psychiatrist with an active teen practice, fluency in the actual research, media presence, and no moral-panic track record — the Damour/Kennedy archetype — optionally paired with a tech-accountability journalist for the reported spine. What's changed is that author risk is now managed as a plan, not a hope: (1) a 90-day, two-track search run in parallel — Track A pursues the named bench (Stanford Brainstorm clinician-researchers behind the Aug 2025 risk assessment; Common Sense Media research leads/advisors on Talk, Trust, and Trade-Offs; Child Mind Institute clinicians already writing its AI guidance; APA task-force members; Character.AI litigation expert witnesses), Track B builds a clinician + journalist co-author pairing so a strong-but-not-celebrity clinician still yields a sellable package; (2) the structure is deliberately load-bearing — named protocols, scripts, the Agreement, the triage section — so the book works with a very good author rather than only a unicorn (the graft from the Bible concept's structural insurance); (3) an institutional co-brand or foreword (Common Sense Media or Child Mind, both currently publishing the stopgap guides) converts the free-content 'rival' into endorsement, launch media, and the school bulk channel, the Safe Word's AARP-as-channel maneuver. Hard gate honored: per the editor's verdict, the proposal does not circulate until a Track A or Track B author is signed — target Labor Day 2026.
marketing_hooks: The Attachment Check-In as a free 10-question online quiz — 'Does your kid have an AI best friend? Most parents don't know' — the awareness-manufacturing engine that solves the unaware-base problem the marketer flagged: the quiz creates the scare, the book sells the solution, every completion is a captured lead.

Morning-show-ready diagnostic: 'Six signs your kid has an AI best friend' plus the 33%-chose-the-bot-over-you stat is a complete GMA/Today segment in one sentence — now backed by a commissioned proprietary parent survey whose release is itself a news event the author owns.

The Family AI Agreement as a viral artifact: a free, downloadable one-page household contract engineered to spread through school newsletters and parent group chats the way Wait Until 8th and Haidt's phone-free pledge did.

Regenerating pegs on a calendar: SB 243 enforcement actions through 2026-27, state copycat bills, the Jan 2027 settlement anniversary, every Common Sense survey update — and a pre-drafted rapid-response op-ed ('the exposé told you what happened; here's what to do tonight') that converts any rival journalist book or fresh litigation headline into this book's media cycle.

Counterprogramming hook for earned media: 'The parenting expert who says DON'T ban your kid's AI girlfriend' — the anti-ban thesis is built for op-ed pages and podcast debate bookings, differentiating every interview from generic AI-panic coverage.

Institutional bulk channel: PTAs, counselors, and districts already buying Anxious Generation class sets need the companion-bot sequel for back-to-school 2027; the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand converts to endorsements, and the family-assignment chapter structure ('read chapter 6 before Sunday') makes two-plus copies per household the natural unit of sale.
refined_concept.open_questions: What refinement cannot fix: (1) Pipeline invisibility — unannounced Big-5 deals can't be searched; a clinician-fronted rival proposal could surface any month, and the only defense is speed, which is a commitment, not a guarantee. (2) The author gate is real — the 90-day two-track plan and load-bearing structure improve the odds, but the named bench is uncontracted, those clinicians have agents and slower clocks, and the editor's condition (no circulation without a signed author) means a failed search kills the deal, correctly. (3) The trade-up bet — that crisis parents pay $28 after free APA/Child Mind guides — is analogous to but not proven by the Haidt precedent; the 72-hour triage section and Breakup protocol are the wager, not the proof. (4) Evidence replication — triangulation and a proprietary survey mitigate but don't eliminate the risk that the 72/52/33 numbers soften; the survey is also uncommissioned until a publisher funds it. (5) Regulatory overshoot — if federal or multi-state law effectively bans under-18 companion access across mainstream platforms too, the migration thesis (use moves rather than stops) is an inference from one Nov 2025 event, not a law of nature. (6) The retitle is a hypothesis — 'Your Kid's AI Best Friend' wins the thumbnail test on paper but is untested against the demographic, and title testing must run before catalog lock.

changes_made: Title soft at thumbnail (marketer: 'could be a communication book'): retitled to 'Your Kid's AI Best Friend: What to Do When the Phone Loves Them Back' — names the buyer, the phenomenon, and the shelf at thumbnail distance; 'The Perfect Listener' is retained inside the book as the named diagnosis ('the perfect-listener problem') and as a tested alternate, with title testing committed before catalog lock.

Author dependency, the consensus kill risk (editor: 'only with a Damour-class clinician signed'; agent: 'a memo, not a proposal'; marketer: 'can't book the morning shows'): converted from a wish list to a plan — a 90-day two-track search (named bench vs. clinician+journalist pairing), structure made deliberately load-bearing so a very good author suffices, an institutional co-brand path (Common Sense/Child Mind), and a hard gate honoring the editor's condition: proposal does not circulate unsigned, target Labor Day 2026.

Single-survey evidence concentration (n=1,060 Common Sense/NORC): added an explicit methodology layer in chapter 2 that triangulates across Pew, Stanford, Internet Matters, and the arXiv literature with stated ranges, plus a commissioned proprietary 2,000-parent survey at proposal stage — turning stat fragility into an authority moat and an owned news event.

Free-content sufficiency (editor kill risk): added the 'Start Here: The First 72 Hours' crisis-triage front section and sharpened the three deliverables no liability-averse nonprofit guide will publish (the Breakup protocol, the platform-combat playbook, the triage section); repositioned the free artifacts (Agreement, quiz) explicitly as funnel rather than substitute.

Exposé preemption by a Character.AI-beat journalist (agent + marketer): pre-positioned the jacket and a pre-drafted rapid-response op-ed as 'after the headlines: what to do Tuesday night,' making a rival exposé function as category marketing (the Good Inside/Anxious Generation coexistence precedent), and committed to a 60-day-to-proposal clock.

Unaware-base pain problem (marketer: 'I have to manufacture the scare before I can sell the solution'): the Attachment Check-In is elevated from a chapter feature to the campaign's lead engine — a free viral online quiz that manufactures the discovery moment and captures leads.

Post-settlement industry-retreat risk (editor): broadened the field-guide chapter from companion apps to all synthetic-intimacy surfaces (mainstream chatbot companion modes, game NPCs, social AIs) with app specifics quarantined in a dated, QR-updated appendix — a dedicated-app retreat now validates the migration thesis instead of voiding the premise.

Moral-panic backlash exposure (open question 6): moved the calibration framework up from chapter 12 to chapter 4 ('Green, Yellow, Red') so credibility is earned before the alarm chapters, structurally front-loading the book's defense against expert criticism.

Concept-first fragility generally: named and flag-planted the protocols (Quiet Audit, Attachment Check-In, Family AI Agreement, the Breakup) as ownable, trademark-able assets a fast-follower must license or awkwardly rename.
Multi-copy economics: scripts and family-meeting chapters rewritten to be assignable ('read chapter 6 before Sunday'), making two-plus copies per household the natural unit of sale.
grafts_from_runners_up: From The Safe Word: the named-protocol flag-plant strategy (a named protocol, once planted, is the hardest position to attack) applied to the Quiet Audit / Check-In / Agreement / Breakup suite; the statistics-rigor-as-authority-moat move (primary sources, stated ranges) now anchors chapter 2; and the convert-the-rival-to-a-channel maneuver (their AARP play) becomes the Common Sense Media / Child Mind co-brand and foreword strategy.

From The Parent Emergency: the crisis-sequenced ER architecture — the new 'Start Here: The First 72 Hours' triage front door that meets the mid-crisis buyer where free web guides currently catch her; and the sibling/co-parent multi-buy structure ('read chapter 6 before Sunday') as a designed conversion mechanic.

From The Off-Ramp: the drug-agnostic/decision-framework defense against landscape churn, applied as the attachment-dynamics-not-app-taxonomy field guide with a dated appendix; and the insurance-combat chapter as earned-media weapon, reborn as the platform-combat playbook in the Red Zone chapter ('the book that teaches you to fight the platform') with model complaint language under SB 243 and the settlements.

From So You Bought a Bible: making the STRUCTURE load-bearing rather than the voice — protocols, scripts, decoder-style field guide, and the Agreement mean the book works with a very good clinician, not only a once-in-a-generation one — the direct structural answer to the author-dependency kill risk.

From When the School Bus Stops Coming: the QR-linked living-update page and edition-cycle design that turns platform/regulatory churn into recurring revised-edition revenue instead of obsolescence, plus the shareable one-page checklist-as-excerpt distribution logic applied to the Agreement and the six-signs list.
unresolved_risks: Pipeline invisibility: unannounced Big-5 deals cannot be searched, and a clinician-fronted rival proposal — the one scenario the protocol-vs-exposé distinction does not defuse — could surface any month; the only mitigation is speed.

The author gate: the 90-day two-track search improves odds but the bench is uncontracted, those clinicians have agents, existing book ideas, and slower clocks; per the editor's own condition, a failed search kills the deal, and the concept is worth nothing without the clinician.

The trade-up bet: no direct proof that parents satisfied by free APA/Child Mind/Common Sense guides convert to a $28 hardcover; the 72-hour triage section and Breakup protocol are the wager, analogous to but not demonstrated by the Anxious Generation precedent.

Evidence replication: triangulation helps, but the headline 72/52/33 numbers still trace primarily to one n=1,060 self-report survey; the proprietary replication survey is unfunded until a publisher commits, and softer replication numbers weaken the urgency case.

Regulatory overshoot: the migration thesis (bans move use rather than stop it) rests on a single November 2025 natural experiment; sweeping federal or multi-state under-18 prohibitions across mainstream platforms could shrink the panic — and the addressable problem — before a back-to-school 2027 pub date.

Title risk: 'Your Kid's AI Best Friend' is a paper hypothesis that fixes the thumbnail problem but is untested against the mothers-35-55 demographic; a title that reads flip rather than urgent could depress the duty-purchase and gift mechanics.

Moral-panic backlash: even with calibration front-loaded, the CHI 2026 literature documents genuine benefits; if the reported spine skews alarmist in execution, the expert class that turned on Haidt makes this book radioactive, and no structural choice can fully constrain an author's tone.
